title: Exam 1
description: OMSCS 6250 Computer Networks — combined question pool for Modules 1–6.

questions.prompt: The modern Internet has its origins in ARPANET, a research network developed in the late 1960s in the United States.
questions.explanation: ARPANET, US, late 1960s. The Internet's predecessor was funded by the US Department of Defense's ARPA and connected research universities; commercial growth followed once NSFNET was retired in the 1990s.

questions.prompt: Layered architectures separate responsibilities across the protocol stack. Which of the following is a key benefit that layering gives the Internet?

options.text: It lets different technologies and protocols evolve independently, as long as each layer keeps the same interface to the layer above.
It guarantees that every packet is delivered in the original order it was sent, with no possibility of loss along the path.
It removes the need for any headers in the data units that are transmitted between hosts on the Internet.
It eliminates the need for routers at the network layer entirely, even in very large interconnected networks.

questions.explanation: Stable interfaces => layers evolve independently. As long as each layer keeps its contract with the layer above, the implementation below can change (e.g., Ethernet -> fiber -> Wi-Fi) without rewriting higher layers.

questions.prompt: In a layered architecture, each layer relies on the service provided by the layer directly above it.
questions.explanation: Each layer uses the layer BELOW, not above. Layering imposes a strict bottom-up service relationship: a layer offers services to the one directly above and consumes services from the one directly below.

questions.prompt: Layering provides modularity, but also introduces trade-offs. Which of the following is the BEST example of duplicated functionality between layers in the Internet protocol stack?

options.text: Wi-Fi performs link-layer error correction on lost or corrupted frames, while TCP separately performs end-to-end retransmission of lost segments at the transport layer.
A router decrements the TTL in the IP header, while the data link layer simultaneously decrements its own per-frame hop counter at every Ethernet switch along the way.
The application layer transmits voltages directly on the wire in modern stacks, fully replacing the physical layer for nearly all routine traffic.
NAT boxes encrypt packets at the network layer to duplicate TLS encryption at the application layer, for added defense in depth.

questions.explanation: Wi-Fi ARQ at L2 + TCP retransmission at L4 -> same job, two layers. Functionality can legitimately repeat in different layers because each addresses a different scope: link-local recovery vs end-to-end delivery.

questions.prompt: Two ISPs may use very different link technologies — one fiber, one cellular — yet still interoperate at the IP layer without either ISP changing its physical layer. This is a direct consequence of the layered design: the lower layers can vary independently, as long as they still expose IP's expected service to the layer above.
questions.explanation: IP waist hides physical/link differences. The narrow IP layer is the only common contract — everything below (copper, fiber, Wi-Fi) and above (TCP, UDP, apps) plugs into IP, so the network supports many media and many apps simultaneously.

questions.prompt: The Internet's Application layer absorbs three OSI layers into one. In practice, what does this mean for application developers?

options.text: Application developers must implement TCP and IP themselves on top of raw sockets in user space.
Each application is required to write its own physical-layer driver and frame format from scratch.
Tasks like data formatting, encryption, and session management end up handled inside the application itself.
The application layer can only send data outward and never receive incoming data from other hosts.

questions.explanation: App handles formatting, encryption, sessions itself. Because the Internet stack merges OSI's Application, Presentation, and Session layers, libraries inside the application — not a separate layer — take care of TLS, JSON formatting, and session state.

questions.prompt: The interface between the application layer and the transport layer in the Internet protocol stack is the MAC address table.
questions.explanation: Socket interface, not MAC table. The boundary between the app and transport layers on Internet hosts is the socket API (e.g., POSIX sockets); MAC tables live inside L2 switches, not at the host's app/transport boundary.

questions.prompt: A process on one host needs to communicate with a process on another host. Which Internet protocol-stack layer is responsible for delivering data between these processes, end-to-end?

options.text: Physical layer
Data link layer
Network layer
Transport layer

questions.explanation: Transport = process-to-process. The network layer gets packets host-to-host; transport adds port numbers so that the right process on each end (a browser vs an SSH daemon) receives its bytes.

questions.prompt: TCP and UDP are the two most common Internet transport protocols. Which statement best contrasts them?

options.text: TCP provides reliable, in-order delivery and congestion control; UDP provides a simpler best-effort service without these guarantees.
TCP is connectionless and offers no reliability; UDP is connection-oriented with strong delivery guarantees built into the protocol.
Both TCP and UDP provide identical guarantees of delivery and ordering; they differ only in header size and option formatting.
UDP provides reliable, ordered delivery with congestion control, while TCP is a best-effort connectionless service.

questions.explanation: TCP: reliable, ordered, congestion-controlled. UDP: best-effort, no guarantees. Apps choose based on need: TCP for streams that must arrive intact (HTTP, SSH); UDP when speed or per-packet control matters (DNS, real-time media).

questions.prompt: The Internet network layer produces a packet called a datagram and is responsible for routing packets across multiple networks based on IP addresses.
questions.explanation: Datagram, IP-based routing. The network layer chops data into datagrams and forwards each one hop-by-hop using its destination IP address; there is no fixed end-to-end path established beforehand.

questions.prompt: What is the role of the data link layer in the Internet protocol stack?

options.text: Provides end-to-end reliability between application processes running on different hosts.
Transfers frames across a single physical link between two adjacent nodes.
Routes packets between distant networks using IP addresses as the addressing scheme.
Encrypts application-layer messages before they are transmitted over the network.

questions.explanation: Data link = frame across ONE hop. L2 moves frames between directly connected nodes (host <-> switch, router <-> router on the same link); spanning multiple links is the network layer's job.

questions.prompt: The physical layer is responsible for routing packets across the Internet based on IP addresses.
questions.explanation: Routing = network layer, not physical. The physical layer just pushes bits onto the medium; choosing where the next hop is happens at L3 based on IP addresses and routing tables.

questions.prompt: Encapsulation works as follows: when data moves down the stack at the sender, each layer adds its own header. When data moves up at the receiver, each layer removes the header added by the corresponding layer at the sender. (Figure: Layered encapsulation across the protocol stack (Module 1))
questions.explanation: Sender adds headers going down; receiver strips them going up. Each layer wraps its peer's view with its own header on transmit, and the receiver's matching layer removes that header before passing the payload upward.

questions.prompt: As an application message moves down the Internet protocol stack and is encapsulated layer by layer, the data unit acquires a different name at each layer. What is the correct name at the network layer?

options.text: Segment
Frame
Datagram
Bit stream

questions.explanation: Network-layer PDU = datagram (segment = transport). Naming changes by layer: bits/frame (L1/L2), datagram or packet (L3), segment (TCP) or datagram (UDP) at L4, message at the app layer.

questions.prompt: Intermediate devices on the Internet implement only the layers they need for forwarding. Which set of layers does a router implement?

options.text: Application, transport, and network only.
Physical and data link only, just like a Layer-2 switch.
All five layers, exactly like an end host that creates application data.
Physical, data link, and network.

questions.explanation: Router = physical + data-link + network. A router must receive bits (L1), parse frames on each link (L2), and make IP forwarding decisions (L3); it does not interpret transport or application payloads.

questions.prompt: The end-to-end principle (Saltzer, Reed, Clark) is a foundational design idea for the Internet. Which statement most accurately captures its core claim?

options.text: Some functions can only be implemented completely and correctly with help from the application at the endpoints; the core should stay simple.
Every layer of the protocol stack must independently re-implement reliability so that applications never see any kind of error anywhere in the stack.
The network core should perform deep packet inspection on every flow in order to provide universal optimization tailored to each application's needs.
Encryption must happen exclusively at the network layer rather than at the application layer, to enforce a uniform security model across the Internet.

questions.explanation: Saltzer–Reed–Clark end-to-end argument: keep smarts at the endpoints; core stays simple. Any function that needs end-to-end semantics (reliability, encryption, integrity) belongs at the hosts, because the network alone cannot guarantee it correctly.

questions.prompt: The end-to-end principle requires that the network core enforce a single, uniform behavior for all applications, regardless of their needs.
questions.explanation: A simple core lets each app choose its own service. Keeping IP minimal means new applications (video, gaming, blockchain) can build whatever transport semantics they need on top — without changing the network.

questions.prompt: A NAT-enabled home router translates between a private home network and the public Internet. When a device inside the home network sends a packet outward, which fields in the packet does the NAT typically rewrite? (Figure: NAT address/port translation (Module 1))

options.text: Only the destination IP address of the outgoing packet.
The source IP address (private -> public), and often the source port as well.
Only the TCP sequence number inside the segment header.
Only the application-layer payload bytes of the packet.

questions.explanation: Source IP (and usually source port) is what a NAT rewrites. NAT lets many private hosts share one public IP by remapping outgoing source addresses (and ports) and reversing the mapping on return traffic.

questions.prompt: A host inside a private NATed network is typically not globally addressable from the public Internet. An outside host usually cannot initiate a connection to it unless the NAT has a mapping or special configuration in place.
questions.explanation: Private hosts behind NAT are not globally addressable from outside. Unsolicited inbound traffic has no NAT state to match, so it gets dropped — which breaks P2P apps and complicates running servers from home.

questions.prompt: Both firewalls and NAT boxes are considered violations of the end-to-end principle. What is the shared reason?

options.text: Both perform encryption on every packet they handle, which the end-to-end principle explicitly prohibits across the stack.
Both run as part of the application on each endpoint, contradicting the strict separation of layers in the network model.
Both sit between the endpoints and actively interfere with the communication — filtering traffic or rewriting addresses — rather than just forwarding.
Both run as background services on the host operating system rather than as elements of the network itself, breaking layering.

questions.explanation: Both NAT and firewalls are middleboxes that interfere between endpoints. They violate the end-to-end model by inspecting/altering traffic in the network core; that complicates protocol evolution but is widely deployed for security and address conservation.

questions.prompt: The Internet protocol stack is often described as a 'pyramid' shape, with many protocols at the top application layer and very few at the bottom physical layer.
questions.explanation: Hourglass shape: a narrow IP waist with wide layers above and below — not a pyramid. Many physical media (Wi-Fi, fiber, cellular) and many applications (web, video, IoT) plug in, but only ONE network-layer protocol (IP) joins them.

questions.prompt: The hourglass shape of the Internet protocol stack helps explain a common pattern in Internet evolution. Which statement best captures that pattern?

options.text: The IP waist is constantly being redesigned by the IETF every few years to match new application requirements.
New link-layer technologies are forbidden so that the hourglass shape of the protocol stack can be preserved indefinitely.
Innovation happens at roughly the same pace at every layer of the protocol stack, regardless of position.
Innovation happens often at the lower layers (e.g., Wi-Fi, fiber) and at the application layer, but the IP/TCP/UDP core changes much more slowly.

questions.explanation: Innovation happens at the edges; the IP/TCP/UDP core stays stable. New apps and link technologies come and go quickly, but the network-layer protocol barely changes — the price for global interoperability.

questions.prompt: Protocol ossification means that an emerging technology at the application layer cannot become widely deployed, because IP locks down the architecture above it.
questions.explanation: Ossification = the core gets hard to replace, yet apps still innovate. So much depends on the current IP/TCP layer that replacing or changing it is nearly impossible (e.g., IPv6 took decades) — while app-layer experimentation remains fast and free.

questions.prompt: EvoArch models a protocol stack as a layered graph: nodes are protocols, edges are dependencies. Which of the following correctly defines 'substrates' and 'products'? (Figure: EvoArch — layered protocol dependency graph (Module 1))

options.text: A protocol's substrates are protocols at the layer below it that it depends on; its products are higher-layer protocols and applications that depend on it.
A protocol's substrates are the higher-layer protocols that consume it; its products are the protocols at the layer below that it depends on.
Substrates and products both refer to the same concept of horizontal competitors operating at the same layer of the stack.
Substrates are simply the physical transmission media a protocol can be sent over, such as copper, fiber, or radio.

questions.explanation: Substrate = the layer below; product = the layer above. In EvoArch a layer's substrates support it, and its products are the things built on top; thinking in substrates/products clarifies why some layers persist.

questions.prompt: In EvoArch, a protocol's evolutionary value is computed by summing the values of its substrates beneath it. Under this definition, TCP would have the same evolutionary value as any other transport protocol that runs on IPv4.
questions.explanation: Evolutionary value depends on the PRODUCTS above, not the substrates below. A layer becomes entrenched because many useful applications/protocols already depend on it (e.g., IP), not because it has many possible substrates.

questions.prompt: EvoArch models competition between protocols at the same layer. Under this model, when do two protocols compete?

options.text: When they were standardized by the same organization in the same decade.
When they share enough of the same products — the same higher-layer protocols or applications that could use them.
When they run over the exact same physical medium and use the same MAC framing rules.
When their packet header lengths happen to be equal at the time of standardization.

questions.explanation: Layers compete when they share enough of the same products. Two protocols serving the same audience compete; the one with deeper product support tends to win and the other declines (e.g., IPX vs IP).

questions.prompt: Suppose a research group designs NextTransport, a new transport protocol that is provably faster and more efficient than TCP under every measured workload. Based on EvoArch's reasoning, why might NextTransport still fail to gain wide adoption on the Internet?

options.text: Because the IETF only standardizes one new transport protocol per decade, and that decade's standardization slot is already taken by another effort.
Because applications and services already depend on TCP. This gives TCP a much higher evolutionary value, and that incumbency advantage protects TCP even against technically superior alternatives.
Because new transport protocols are not allowed to run over IPv4 without explicit permission from each network operator along the path.
Because NextTransport's header is incompatible with IP routing and cannot be forwarded by Internet routers regardless of its underlying performance.

questions.explanation: Incumbents are protected by their high evolutionary value. Once a protocol has many products above it, replacing it requires migrating all of them — which is why TCP/IP, HTTP, and DNS are extremely hard to displace.

questions.prompt: EvoArch uses a 'layer generality' parameter to model how protocols at different layers spread or specialize. Which statement most accurately describes what this parameter captures?

options.text: It is measured at runtime from observed traffic volume, with lower-layer protocols automatically receiving higher values when they carry more bytes.
It is a network-management policy that the IETF enforces on a quarterly basis to keep the protocol ecosystem in balance over time.

It is a model input parameter capturing how 'general' a layer's service is — typically higher at lower layers (one substrate serves many products) and lower at higher layers (each protocol is more specialized).
It is the number of physical interfaces present in any given deployed protocol stack across the Internet at any moment.

questions.explanation: Lower layers are more general — they support more diverse products above. IP supports a huge variety of transports and apps; an application protocol like HTTP supports a much smaller, more specialised set of higher-level uses.

questions.prompt: A learning bridge is a Layer-2 device that builds its forwarding table by inspecting the source MAC address of each arriving frame, along with the port on which the frame arrived. (Figure: Learning bridge — observing source addresses (Module 1))
questions.explanation: Bridge learns from the source MAC and the port the frame arrived on. As frames pass through, the bridge records 'MAC X is reachable via port P' so it can later forward unicast frames to X only on port P, not flood them.

questions.prompt: A learning bridge maintains a forwarding table that maps MAC addresses to ports. When a frame arrives whose destination MAC is already in the table, what does the bridge do?

options.text: It discards the frame, because the destination is already known to exist elsewhere in the network.
It broadcasts the frame on every port, just to be safe in case the table entry is stale.
It forwards the frame only on the port that leads toward the destination.
It sends an ICMP echo request to the destination first to verify the host is still reachable.

questions.explanation: Known destination -> forward only on the learned port. Once a destination MAC is in the table, the bridge stops flooding and sends the frame out only on the correct port, saving bandwidth on the other segments.

questions.prompt: Imagine a hypothetical learning-bridge design that, instead of FLOODING frames whose destination MAC is not yet in the table, simply DROPS them. What is the most important consequence of this change?

options.text: The bridge would forward traffic noticeably faster, because no port duplication is needed for any frame on the local segment.
The bridge would become a Layer-3 device instead of a Layer-2 device, requiring it to start parsing IP headers on every frame.
The network would become more secure, because broadcast traffic would no longer leak across switches within the same segment.

The bridge would never have an opportunity to learn the destination's location. It populates its forwarding table by observing reply frames from that destination — and those replies never come if the original frame was dropped.

questions.explanation: Drop instead of flood => no reply => no learning. Learning bridges depend on observing return traffic; if an unknown destination is dropped rather than flooded, the destination never replies and the bridge can never learn its location.

questions.prompt: Network designers add redundant links between bridges to improve reliability. Because Layer-2 frames have no hop-limit field like IP's TTL, these redundant links can let a single broadcast frame loop and multiply indefinitely if no protection is in place.
questions.explanation: L2 frames have no TTL; loops circulate forever. Unlike IP packets, Ethernet frames have no built-in expiration — a topological loop in L2 lets a broadcast frame multiply and saturate the entire bridged network.

questions.prompt: Why is the Spanning Tree Algorithm needed in a bridged network that has redundant links? (Figure: Spanning-tree-algorithm execution among bridges (Module 1))

options.text: To dynamically increase link capacity by aggregating multiple redundant links into a single high-bandwidth virtual link.
To compress Ethernet frame headers before they cross redundant links, reducing the per-frame overhead at line rate.
To automatically assign IP addresses to each bridge in the topology so that the bridges can communicate with each other.
To remove cycles from the forwarding topology so frames don't loop indefinitely, while keeping the physical redundancy intact.

questions.explanation: Spanning Tree removes cycles by computing a tree, while preserving the underlying physical redundancy. Redundant links stay physically connected (in case a primary fails), but STA logically disables them so the active topology stays loop-free.

questions.prompt: Why does the Internet need a transport layer between the application and network layers?

options.text: The network layer offers best-effort delivery with no guarantees; transport adds reliability and process-to-process delivery.
The router fabric cannot accept packets unless transport-layer headers re-write IP source and destination addresses along the path at every intermediate hop.
The network layer can only deliver fixed-size physical frames, so all segmentation, reassembly, and reordering of payloads must happen above it in a separate layer.
Application code cannot itself construct IP packets and depends on transport to also compute IP routes, manage neighbor tables, and select egress interfaces.

questions.explanation: Network = best-effort host-to-host; transport = process-to-process + reliability. The transport layer adds what IP alone doesn't give: port numbers (so the right process gets the bytes) and, for TCP, retransmission and ordering on top of IP's lossy datagram service.

questions.prompt: An IP address alone is sufficient to deliver an incoming packet to the correct application process on a host, without using port numbers.
questions.explanation: IP gets you to the host, not the process. Without port numbers in the transport header, the OS can't decide whether a packet belongs to your browser, your SSH client, or your mail daemon — all of them share the same IP.

questions.prompt: A receiving host uses connectionless (UDP) demultiplexing. Which field(s) does it use to identify the correct socket?

options.text: Only the source IP address and the source port number carried in the segment header by the sender of the datagram.
The destination IP plus destination port (the UDP socket).
The full four-tuple of source IP, source port, destination IP, and destination port — exactly as TCP would use to identify a unique connection-oriented socket.
The TCP sequence number embedded in the segment header, taken modulo 2^32 to give an offset into the receiver's per-socket buffer.

questions.explanation: UDP demux key = (dest IP, dest port). UDP is connectionless and stateless: any datagram with the same destination two-tuple lands on the same socket, regardless of who sent it.

questions.prompt: A web server listens on port 80 and serves many simultaneous TCP clients. How can it demultiplex incoming segments that all carry destination port 80 to the right socket?

options.text: By rotating clients through a single shared listening socket in strict round-robin order, one segment per client per scheduler tick.
By renegotiating the destination port number for each new client during the TCP three-way handshake, using a special option in the SYN.
Using the four-tuple (source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port).
By assigning every client a unique destination IP alias at the server side and binding each one to a separate kernel-level TCP socket.

questions.explanation: TCP demux key = 4-tuple. Even when many clients share destination (server IP, port 80), each client's connection is unique because (src IP, src port) differs — the kernel uses all four fields to pick the right socket.

questions.prompt: In the figure, two hosts initiate HTTP sessions to the same web server B on port 80. If both clients happen to pick the same source port number, how does server B still keep the connections separate? (Figure: Multiple HTTP sessions to one web server (Module 2))

options.text: It refuses one of the two connections because source ports must always be globally unique across the public Internet for every concurrent TCP flow worldwide.
It demultiplexes by inspecting the URL inside the application-layer payload of the GET request — the URL is what truly identifies a unique HTTP session.
It assigns each client a freshly-allocated destination port at handshake time, signalled with a TCP option attached to the SYNACK so that two clients never share a destination port.
It uses source IP (part of the 4-tuple) to distinguish the two sockets.

questions.explanation: Source IP breaks the tie. If two clients happen to choose the same source port, the 4-tuple still differs in the source-IP component — so the server's TCP can distinguish the two connections.

questions.prompt: A single UDP server socket is bound to (192.0.2.7, 5060). It receives a datagram from (203.0.113.4, 41000) and shortly after another datagram from (203.0.113.9, 41000). Which statement is correct?

options.text: Both datagrams are delivered to the same socket and the application can distinguish them by reading the sender address with recvfrom().
The host's UDP layer rejects the second datagram because two different sources cannot share the same source port.
The host kernel creates a new socket on the fly for the second sender, transparently to the application.
UDP demultiplexes using the four-tuple, so each unique source IP+port pair is mapped to a different listening socket on the server.

questions.explanation: UDP socket is bound to dest (IP,port) only. Both senders land on the same socket; the application reads sender identity from recvfrom()'s return values, since UDP itself doesn't demux by source.

questions.prompt: A user's laptop opens two TCP connections from the same browser to the same web server (203.0.113.10, 443). The kernel assigns source ports 51500 and 51501. Which statement explains why the server can keep the two connections distinct?

options.text: The destination IP differs across the two connections because the server uses anycast.
TCP demultiplexing uses the full four-tuple, and the two connections differ in their source-port component (51500 vs 51501).
TCP gives every new connection a unique destination port number negotiated during the three-way handshake, making the destination ports the distinguishing field.
The browser embeds a connection ID in the TCP options field that the server uses as a primary demultiplexing key in the kernel.

questions.explanation: Different source ports => different 4-tuples. The kernel assigns ephemeral ports per connection (51500 vs 51501), so even though everything else matches, the two flows demux to separate sockets.

questions.prompt: A browser opens six parallel TCP connections to fetch images from one CDN edge. From the server's perspective, what makes the six connections separable at the transport layer?

options.text: The browser sets a unique TLS Server Name Indication value per connection, which the kernel uses as an extra demultiplexing key beyond ports.
UDP encapsulation underneath HTTP gives each of the six connections a different multiplexing channel ID at the network layer.
Each of the six connections has a distinct source port chosen by the client, so their four-tuples differ even though source IP, destination IP, and destination port are identical.
The CDN server allocates a unique destination port per connection and returns it in the SYNACK so that each browser-side request lands on its own listening port.

questions.explanation: Six distinct source ports => six distinct 4-tuples. The browser opens parallel connections by letting the OS allocate a fresh ephemeral source port for each — same dest, different source-port component.

questions.prompt: Two distinct UDP applications on the same host can listen on the same destination port without conflict, because UDP uses the full four-tuple — including source address — as its demultiplexing key.
questions.explanation: UDP demuxes only on (dest IP, dest port). Two processes binding the same UDP port on the same host conflict — the kernel can't route incoming datagrams to two different sockets that match the same 2-tuple.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT a reason that some real-time applications prefer UDP over TCP?

options.text: UDP avoids the three-way handshake delay before sending data.
UDP does not impose congestion-control back-off on the sender.
UDP gives the application direct control over when data is sent.
UDP guarantees in-order, loss-free delivery for time-sensitive flows like RTP or VoIP, with retransmissions of any dropped packets to maintain perfect fidelity.

questions.explanation: UDP gives NO guarantees — that's the opposite of why real-time apps pick it. Real-time apps choose UDP precisely to skip TCP's handshake, ordering, and back-off; they tolerate loss rather than tolerate the latency cost of guaranteed delivery.

questions.prompt: The UDP header is 64 bits long and contains a source port, destination port, length, and checksum field.
questions.explanation: UDP header = 4 fields x 16 bits = 64 bits total. Source port, destination port, length, checksum — minimal, by design, so UDP adds almost no overhead over raw IP.

questions.prompt: Most DNS queries are sent over UDP rather than TCP. Which is the BEST single reason for that design choice?

options.text: DNS queries and responses are typically small enough to fit in a single packet, and avoiding TCP's connection-setup handshake saves a full round-trip per lookup.
UDP gives DNS strong delivery guarantees that TCP does not provide because DNS itself enforces no application-layer retransmission.
TCP packets are systematically dropped by recursive resolvers; only UDP traffic is forwarded to the authoritative servers.
DNS resolvers require congestion-control rate limiting on every lookup, which TCP does not provide but UDP does at the kernel level.

questions.explanation: DNS = small message + no handshake savings. Most DNS queries and replies fit in one packet; skipping TCP's three-way handshake saves a full RTT per lookup, which dominates web page load time.

questions.prompt: An online multiplayer game sends fast-paced position updates 60 times per second. Which transport protocol is the more natural choice, and why?

options.text: TCP, because the game absolutely needs in-order, lossless delivery of every position update so that no client ever falls behind on the latest game state.
UDP, because losing one position update is acceptable; the next update arrives quickly anyway, and TCP's retransmissions/back-off would add stale data plus jitter.
TCP, because UDP cannot pass through any of the home-router NATs that are typical in residential broadband deployments at scale.
UDP, because the game needs guaranteed in-order delivery without any congestion control to keep the round-trip time predictable for the player.

questions.explanation: Stale data is worse than missing data. Position updates 60x/sec self-correct; TCP would buffer late packets to deliver in order, adding latency and jitter — exactly what real-time games can't tolerate.

questions.prompt: VoIP traffic generally uses UDP rather than TCP. Which property of TCP makes it a POOR fit for an interactive voice call?

options.text: TCP requires a third-party authoritative time service for each call, which prevents real-time audio delivery in the absence of synchronized clocks among the participants.
TCP cannot carry the small audio payload sizes that VoIP codecs typically produce; UDP is the only transport that supports fragments smaller than 1500 bytes on the wire.
A TCP segment loss triggers retransmission, and any retransmitted audio sample arrives too late to be played out — it would only add latency without benefit.
UDP, unlike TCP, automatically applies forward error correction across consecutive packets to recover from loss, which audio codecs can leverage natively.

questions.explanation: Retransmitted voice = useless voice. By the time TCP recovers a lost audio frame, it's already past its playback deadline; better to skip it and keep latency low than wait and play out-of-time samples.

questions.prompt: A user uploads a 4 GB ZIP archive over the Internet to cloud storage. Which transport protocol is the natural fit, and why?

options.text: UDP, because file uploads are tolerant of loss and reordering and benefit from skipping the three-way handshake to minimize startup delay.
TCP, because UDP packets are dropped by 80% of public ISPs as a matter of network-management policy and cannot be used for any file transfer at all.
UDP, because TCP's window-scaled rate limits would prevent a multi-gigabyte transfer from completing on a typical home broadband link in a reasonable time.
TCP, because every byte must arrive intact and in order, and TCP gives both reliability and congestion control without the application reimplementing them.

questions.explanation: Bulk data needs every byte, in order. TCP gives lossless, ordered delivery plus congestion control for free — exactly what a file transfer needs and what UDP would force the application to reimplement itself.

questions.prompt: A stock-exchange feed broadcasts tick updates to many subscribers continuously. Which trade-off best argues for using UDP in this design?

options.text: Losing a single stale tick is acceptable since a fresher tick will follow shortly, and avoiding TCP's per-flow congestion control keeps the publish-side latency consistent and predictable.
UDP guarantees in-order arrival of every tick to every subscriber, which is essential for any market data feed because subscribers must never see the prices in the wrong order.
UDP, unlike TCP, has built-in multicast support that lets the exchange deliver each tick to every subscriber with zero protocol overhead compared to any reliable transport.
TCP cannot be used at all because exchange firewalls block all connection-oriented traffic by policy to prevent attackers from establishing long-lived sessions inward.

questions.explanation: Stale tick = useless tick, like VoIP audio. A fresher price will arrive in milliseconds, so retransmitting an old tick wastes bandwidth and adds latency; predictable per-packet timing matters more than zero loss.

questions.prompt: In the third step of the TCP three-way handshake, the client sends an acknowledgment with the SYN bit set to 0.
questions.explanation: ACK only — no new SYN. The third segment is the client's final acknowledgment; the SYN bit is cleared because connection establishment was negotiated in segments 1 and 2.

questions.prompt: Based on the handshake shown in the figure, which field does the server place in the acknowledgment field of its SYNACK? (Figure: TCP three-way handshake (Module 2))

options.text: Its own randomly chosen server_isn, identifying the half of the connection that the server itself controls and uses for its outbound stream of data segments back toward the client.
client_isn + 1 (acknowledging the client's SYN).
Zero, because no actual application-layer data has been received yet at the server when the SYNACK is constructed and dispatched back to the client.
The TCP MSS value negotiated for the connection, which doubles as the initial sequence-number basis for the server's side of the bidirectional data exchange.

questions.explanation: ACK = client_isn + 1. The server's SYNACK acknowledges the client's SYN by setting the ACK field to one past the client's chosen initial sequence number, indicating 'I expect client_isn+1 next.'

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the Go-back-N strategy described in the lesson?

options.text: The receiver individually ACKs every out-of-order packet it sees, and the sender retransmits only the single packet that was missing from the sequence.
The sender transmits one packet and then waits for its ACK to arrive before being allowed to send the next packet, completely serializing the data flow.
The receiver ACKs the most recently in-order packet; the sender retransmits from the missing packet onward.
The sender retransmits only after a hard retransmission timeout fires; under Go-back-N it never reacts to duplicate ACKs in any way, even repeated ones.

questions.explanation: Cumulative ACK of last in-order; sender retransmits from missing onward. The receiver doesn't buffer out-of-order packets, so the sender resends the entire window starting at the lost one — simple but wasteful.

questions.prompt: How many duplicate ACKs does TCP wait for before triggering fast retransmit?

options.text: One duplicate ACK — TCP retransmits as soon as the very first duplicate arrives at the sender.
TCP fast retransmit never triggers on duplicate ACKs; it always waits for the retransmission timeout to fire.
Two duplicate ACKs.
Three duplicate ACKs.

questions.explanation: Three duplicate ACKs trigger fast retransmit. RFC 5681's standard heuristic: three duplicates is strong evidence of a single packet loss (not just reordering), so retransmit immediately without waiting for the timeout.

questions.prompt: In the Go-back-N scenario in the figure, packet 7 is lost and the receiver continues to receive packets 8, 9, 10. Which ACKs does the receiver send for those later packets? (Figure: Go-back-N with packet 7 lost (Module 2))

options.text: Duplicate ACKs for packet 6 (the last in-order packet); packets 8–10 are discarded.
Nothing — the receiver stays completely silent until packet 7 has been retransmitted and successfully received, only then resuming its acknowledgment stream.
A single cumulative ACK 10, because all subsequent packets in the window have arrived intact and the receiver's protocol can summarize them in one ACK.
ACK 8, ACK 9, ACK 10 — the receiver individually acknowledges each correctly received packet using the next-expected sequence number in each separate ACK segment.

questions.explanation: GBN: receiver discards everything after the gap. With cumulative ACKs only, the receiver keeps re-acking the last in-order packet (6) for every subsequent arrival, drops 8/9/10, and waits for retransmission.

questions.prompt: In the fast-retransmit figure, the sender receives three duplicate ACKs after packet 7 is lost. What number do those duplicate ACKs carry, and what does the sender do? (Figure: Fast retransmit triggered by three duplicate ACKs (Module 2))

options.text: They ACK packet 7 itself, because that is the packet the receiver still wants; the sender takes no action since seven was already acknowledged in the prior duplicate-ACK stream.
They ACK packet 6; sender fast-retransmits packet 7 without waiting for the timeout.
They ACK packet 10, signalling that all later packets arrived; the sender concludes the connection is healthy and continues sending new data segments forward.
They are SYNACKs that reset the connection because three retransmissions in a row indicate the receiver thinks the connection has been lost.

questions.explanation: Dup ACKs ACK the last in-order packet = 6. Three duplicates ACKing 6 mean 'I'm still missing 7'; the sender doesn't wait for the timer and immediately retransmits packet 7 (fast retransmit).

questions.prompt: What does the TCP receive window (rwnd) represent?

options.text: The number of packets per second the network path can deliver without loss, as measured by the sender during slow start.
The number of bytes the sender has transmitted but not yet acknowledged, sometimes called 'bytes in flight'.
The amount of free space currently available in the receiver's buffer.
The maximum segment size (MSS) negotiated during the TCP three-way handshake.

questions.explanation: rwnd = free space in the receiver's buffer. The receiver advertises rwnd in every ACK so the sender knows how much it can transmit before risking the receiver's buffer overflowing.

questions.prompt: When the receiver advertises rwnd = 0, TCP fixes the resulting deadlock by having the sender continue to send small (one-byte) segments so it learns when buffer space frees up.
questions.explanation: Zero-window probe keeps the connection alive. If rwnd hits 0, the sender's persist timer fires and sends a 1-byte probe; the receiver's ACK carries the updated rwnd, breaking the deadlock without needing a separate signal.

questions.prompt: Which statement correctly contrasts end-to-end and network-assisted congestion control as described in the lesson?

options.text: End-to-end congestion control relies on routers sending explicit feedback such as ICMP source quench messages to the sender after every congestion event.
Network-assisted congestion control requires the receiver to infer congestion from RTT measurements alone, without any cooperation from intermediate devices in the network core.
End-to-end and network-assisted approaches both forbid routers from ever marking packets, even with explicit congestion notification (ECN).
TCP traditionally uses end-to-end congestion control, inferring congestion from packet loss and delay.

questions.explanation: TCP = end-to-end: infer from loss/delay. The traditional model gets no help from routers — TCP deduces congestion from packet drops and RTT growth, treating the network as a black box.

questions.prompt: Which signal did the earliest TCP implementations use to infer congestion, and why?

options.text: Packet loss, because TCP already had to detect and handle loss for reliability.
ICMP source-quench messages, because routers always send them under load and the messages are highly reliable indicators of incipient congestion along the path.
The receive-window size, because rwnd directly reflects router queue depth at every hop along the network path.
Round-trip time variance, because delay is the most stable and least noisy congestion signal in real networks.

questions.explanation: Free signal: loss-detection was already there. Early TCP needed loss-detection for reliability, so reusing it as a congestion signal added no extra mechanism — congestion-induced drops were already visible to the sender.

questions.prompt: TCP Reno treats three duplicate ACKs and a retransmission timeout as DIFFERENT events. Which interpretation explains why dup-ACKs are considered a less severe congestion signal than a timeout?

options.text: Duplicate ACKs always come from a different congested router than the one that causes timeouts, so the duplicate-ACK signal is geographically less severe.
Duplicate ACKs prove that later packets are still reaching the receiver — i.e., the pipe is mostly working; a timeout suggests the path may be entirely blocked.
Timeouts are generated only by the receiver-side flow-control logic and are unrelated to network congestion, while duplicate ACKs come from the routers themselves.
Duplicate ACKs are sent in response to receiver buffer overflow, not network loss, so they signal an application-layer issue rather than a transport-layer congestion event.

questions.explanation: Dup ACKs prove later packets still arriving => pipe partly working. A timeout means the sender hears nothing — the path may be wholly broken; dup ACKs say 'one packet was lost but the rest got through,' so it's a milder signal.

questions.prompt: Reno's response to a triple-duplicate-ACK event differs sharply from its response to a timeout. Which choice describes the rationale for this asymmetry?

options.text: The protocol designers found that halving cwnd was too aggressive in early experiments, so they chose to halve only on dup-ACK and reset on timeout to push receivers into faster discovery of available bandwidth in the bottleneck queue.

Halving cwnd on a triple dup-ACK is a way to mimic legacy stop-and-wait protocols; resetting on a timeout exists for backward compatibility with experimental T/TCP, which used a non-standard cwnd state machine.

Halving cwnd on a triple dup-ACK matches the probabilistic estimate that one segment was lost while the rest of the window arrived; resetting cwnd on a timeout reflects the much higher risk that the path is severely impaired.

Triple duplicate ACKs always arrive from network-internal ECN-aware switches, while timeouts are produced by the sender's local kernel — both behaviours reflect entirely different congestion-signaling code paths and ideological assumptions about packet loss.

questions.explanation: One lost segment vs path possibly down => different cuts. Halving cwnd assumes a single drop in a mostly-healthy pipe; resetting to 1 MSS assumes the path may be severely impaired, since no ACKs at all reached the sender.

questions.prompt: Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN) lets routers mark packets to signal congestion without dropping them, giving TCP a 'softer' signal that ACKs the same kind of information as packet loss but earlier in the build-up.
questions.explanation: ECN = router marks instead of drops, earlier softer signal. ECN-aware routers set the CE bit on packets they would otherwise drop, letting senders react to congestion before any actual loss occurs.

questions.prompt: The amount of unacknowledged data a TCP sender may have outstanding is bounded by min(cwnd, rwnd).
questions.explanation: Outstanding bytes <= min(cwnd, rwnd). The sender obeys whichever ceiling is lower — network capacity (cwnd) or receiver buffer (rwnd) — to avoid either congesting the network or overrunning the receiver.

questions.prompt: TCP Reno's cwnd is currently 16 packets when a triple-duplicate-ACK loss event occurs. What does cwnd become immediately afterward?

options.text: One packet — Reno resets to the initial window on any loss event of any kind, including duplicate ACKs.
17 packets, because Reno continues to grow additively on duplicate ACKs.
15 packets — Reno decreases additively by one on every duplicate-ACK group, mirroring its additive-increase rule in reverse.
8 packets.

questions.explanation: Triple dup ACK = halve cwnd. Reno's mild-loss response: 16/2 = 8, then resume additive increase from there. Multiplicative decrease, additive increase = AIMD.

questions.prompt: TCP Reno distinguishes two loss signals. How does it react to each?

options.text: Triple dup ACK halves cwnd (mild); timeout resets cwnd to the initial window (severe).
On a timeout, cwnd is doubled as an aggressive probe for capacity; on a triple duplicate ACK, cwnd is halved as a conservative back-off.
Both triple duplicate ACKs and a timeout cut cwnd in half, since Reno treats the two signals identically regardless of severity.
Triple duplicate ACKs reset cwnd all the way to one packet; a timeout merely halves cwnd as a softer response.

questions.explanation: Dup ACK -> halve; timeout -> reset to 1 MSS. Reno treats the two signals very differently: dup ACK suggests one drop in a still-functioning path, while timeout suggests the path may be entirely broken.

questions.prompt: The figure shows TCP Reno's cwnd over time. Why does the plot have a sawtooth shape? (Figure: TCP Reno cwnd sawtooth (Module 2))

options.text: cwnd grows exponentially without bound until the receiver's rwnd is fully exhausted at the application layer, at which point cwnd drops to zero and the connection idles.
cwnd grows additively by one packet per RTT, then is halved on a triple-duplicate-ACK loss event, producing repeated rise-and-cut cycles.
cwnd oscillates because the receiver advertises rwnd in a sinusoidal pattern that the sender mirrors back, producing a wave-like teeth shape over the lifetime of the connection.
cwnd actually remains constant throughout; the saw-tooth shape on the plot is an artifact of variable RTT estimates being misinterpreted as window changes by the visualization tool.

questions.explanation: +1 per RTT additive, /2 on triple dup ACK => saw-tooth. AIMD's slow rise and sharp cut produces the characteristic sawtooth, which on average tracks the bottleneck capacity.

questions.prompt: In the figure, cwnd grows up to the labeled peak of 100 and then suddenly drops to 50 in one step. Which event most likely occurred at that peak? (Figure: TCP cwnd evolution — slow-start, AIMD, triple-dup, timeout (Module 2))

options.text: A retransmission timeout, because cwnd was simply reduced to roughly half of its previous value rather than being reset all the way to the smallest possible window.
The receiver re-advertised rwnd = 50, forcing the sender to immediately throttle cwnd down to the new flow-control ceiling indicated by the receiver.
A triple-duplicate-ACK loss event, since cwnd was halved (100 → 50) rather than reset to 1 MSS.
The application gracefully closed the TCP connection at that exact moment, which always halves cwnd before fully tearing down the local socket.

questions.explanation: Halving = triple-dup-ACK signal. The drop from 100 to 50 is exactly multiplicative decrease / 2 — Reno's response to a mild loss event, not a full reset to 1 MSS.

questions.prompt: Later in the figure, cwnd climbs back up to 100 again but then drops ALL the way down to 1 MSS (not just to 50). What event explains this? (Figure: TCP cwnd evolution — slow-start, AIMD, triple-dup, timeout (Module 2))

options.text: A flow-control deadlock between sender and receiver, which always pins cwnd at exactly 1 MSS until rwnd is unfrozen by the next zero-window probe sequence.
A triple-duplicate-ACK event in Reno; the protocol always cuts cwnd more aggressively whenever three duplicate ACKs are reordered, sometimes all the way to 1 MSS.
The receiver advertised rwnd = 1, capping the sender at exactly one segment of in-flight data and forcing cwnd downward to match the new ceiling.
A retransmission timeout — the more severe loss signal: cwnd is reset to the initial window (≈ 1 MSS), reflecting Reno's worst-case assumption about path connectivity.

questions.explanation: Reset to 1 MSS = timeout signal. A full collapse to the initial window indicates Reno saw no ACKs at all for a full RTO and conservatively assumed the path may be down.

questions.prompt: After a triple-dup-ACK halves cwnd (e.g., 100 → 50 or 80 → 40), what growth rule does Reno use in the climbing slope visible in the figure right after the cut? (Figure: TCP cwnd evolution — slow-start, AIMD, triple-dup, timeout (Module 2))

options.text: Additive — cwnd increases by approximately 1 MSS per RTT (congestion-avoidance mode).
Exponential — cwnd doubles every RTT in slow-start fashion until reaching the previous peak again before the next loss event occurs in the connection's flight.
Multiplicative — cwnd is multiplied by approximately 1.5 every RTT until ssthresh is reached or the next loss event interrupts the climb in the flight.
Constant — cwnd is pinned at its post-halving value (e.g., 50 or 40) until another loss is detected, at which point it drops further by another multiplicative factor.

questions.explanation: Additive: +1 MSS per RTT (congestion avoidance). After the cut, Reno enters congestion-avoidance mode, growing linearly to gently probe for available capacity without immediately recreating the loss.

questions.prompt: Looking at the figure: the leftmost curve grows EXPONENTIALLY from 1 MSS up to the slow-start threshold (~50), then growth becomes LINEAR. After a TIMEOUT (cwnd → 1 MSS), exponential growth restarts. When is Reno in slow start vs congestion avoidance? (Figure: TCP cwnd evolution — slow-start, AIMD, triple-dup, timeout (Module 2))

options.text: Slow start runs only once, at the very start of the connection; once it ends, Reno is in congestion-avoidance mode forever, regardless of any loss events or timeouts that occur.
Slow start runs whenever cwnd is below ssthresh; congestion avoidance runs when cwnd ≥ ssthresh.
Slow start runs whenever cwnd is above ssthresh; congestion avoidance runs whenever cwnd is below it, the exact reverse of the convention shown in the figure.
Slow start and congestion avoidance both run continuously in parallel and average together; the visible sawtooth is the result of their interaction at the sender's pacing rate.

questions.explanation: cwnd < ssthresh => slow start (exponential); cwnd >= ssthresh => congestion avoidance (linear). ssthresh marks the boundary between aggressive ramp-up after a reset and cautious probing near the suspected capacity.

questions.prompt: Why does the cwnd curve in the figure repeatedly climb up to a peak (e.g., 100) and then get cut back, instead of growing unbounded? (Figure: TCP cwnd evolution — slow-start, AIMD, triple-dup, timeout (Module 2))

options.text: Because TCP's additive increase is so slow that no flow ever reaches the bottleneck; the visible peaks in the figure are unrelated to actual network capacity and are purely artifacts of the plot's vertical scale.
Because the receiver-advertised rwnd is statically set to half the bottleneck capacity, capping the sawtooth from above at a value the sender's cwnd can never exceed even momentarily.
Because cwnd grows until the bottleneck queue overflows and a packet is lost; the resulting dup-ACK or timeout cuts cwnd, so the average sending rate stays near the link's capacity.
Because routers advertise their capacity to the sender via ICMP source-quench messages, telling Reno exactly when to stop and start the cwnd cycle that produces the saw-tooth shape.

questions.explanation: AIMD peaks track the bottleneck. cwnd grows until it overruns the queue, a packet is lost, and cwnd is cut — so the average sending rate oscillates around the actual link capacity rather than running away.

questions.prompt: During slow start, TCP increases cwnd linearly (by one packet per RTT) rather than exponentially.
questions.explanation: Slow start doubles cwnd per RTT (exponential), not linear. The name is historical: it's slow compared to sending the full pipe immediately, but it ramps up much faster than congestion avoidance.

questions.prompt: In the slow-start figure, what is happening to cwnd as each round of ACKs arrives? (Figure: Slow start exponential growth (Module 2))

options.text: cwnd stays constant for the entirety of slow start until the slow-start threshold ssthresh expires; only then does the sender begin to actually increase its window size at all.
cwnd grows by one packet per RTT (linear additive increase), then continues to grow at the same modest pace once congestion-avoidance mode begins, with no acceleration.
cwnd is halved each RTT to probe for capacity gently, since slow start is designed as a cautious back-off mechanism rather than as an exponential ramp-up phase.
cwnd is incremented by one for every ACK received, so it roughly doubles each RTT (exponential growth) until reaching ssthresh.

questions.explanation: +1 MSS per ACK => doubles each RTT. Every ACK bumps cwnd by one segment; over one RTT you receive ~cwnd ACKs, so cwnd doubles — exponential growth until ssthresh is hit.

questions.prompt: Two TCP connections with equal RTT sharing a bottleneck link of capacity R will, under AIMD, converge toward each receiving approximately R/2.
questions.explanation: AIMD with equal RTTs => both converge to R/2. Equal RTTs grow cwnd at equal rates; multiplicative decrease equalises the cut; the equilibrium is the fair share.

questions.prompt: The fairness graph plots throughput of connection 1 vs. connection 2 sharing a link of capacity R. Why does AIMD push the operating point toward the intersection of the equal-share line and the full-utilization line? (Figure: AIMD convergence to fairness (Module 2))

options.text: Additive increase moves both connections equally toward the capacity line; multiplicative decrease moves them along a line toward the origin (the equal-share line), so iterations converge to that intersection.
AIMD always picks the connection with the larger throughput and starves it until both shares equalize, regardless of any other policy or queue management at the router along the bottleneck path.
Routers explicitly signal each sender how much to slow down so that perfect fairness is achieved across all sharing connections at any given moment without any randomization in the schedule.
TCP randomizes cwnd at the start of every single RTT in such a way that, statistically over many rounds, both flows happen to average out to exactly R/2 share each by pure chance alone.

questions.explanation: AI moves both up the same diagonal; MD pulls them toward the origin along the equal-share line. Geometrically, iterating these two moves converges to the intersection of equal-share and full-utilization lines — the fair operating point.

questions.prompt: Why might TCP be unfair between two connections that share a bottleneck?

options.text: TCP gives whichever connection started first a permanently larger cwnd by design — first arrival permanently locks in a higher fair share by protocol mandate.
Connections with smaller RTT grow cwnd faster, and apps that open many parallel TCP connections get a disproportionate share of the bottleneck.
TCP always allocates available bandwidth proportionally to the destination port number, so flows to higher-numbered ports systematically receive more capacity than those to lower ports.
Connections with larger RTT are given strict priority service by routers along the bottleneck path, displacing shorter-RTT flows in the queue.

questions.explanation: Smaller RTT grows cwnd faster + N parallel flows get Nx. RTT bias: per-RTT additive increase favours short paths; and an app opening many connections gets a per-flow share each, multiplying its bandwidth.

questions.prompt: TCP CUBIC uses W(t) = C(t - K)^3 + Wmax. What does the variable t represent?

options.text: The number of ACKs received since the connection was first established, summed across all RTTs to give a cumulative count of forward progress.
The current RTT estimate, expressed in milliseconds and refreshed continuously by exponentially weighted moving averages over every newly observed sample.
The time elapsed since the last loss event (makes CUBIC RTT-fair).
The number of duplicate ACKs received since the most recent Wmax checkpoint, used as a substitute for true wall-clock time in CUBIC.

questions.explanation: t = wall-clock time since the last loss event. Tying growth to real time, not RTT-count, is what makes CUBIC RTT-fair: two flows with different RTTs progress at the same wall-clock pace.

questions.prompt: Looking at the CUBIC window-growth curve in the figure, why is the growth aggressive far from Wmax but slow near Wmax? (Figure: TCP CUBIC window growth function (Module 2))

options.text: Because the receiver advertises a progressively smaller rwnd as cwnd approaches Wmax, which throttles the sender from above and produces the visible slow-down in the plot at the inflection.
Because CUBIC is required by RFC to mimic Reno's linear additive-increase rate exactly when near Wmax for backwards compatibility with legacy slow-start implementations in the kernel.
Because RTT measurements become unreliable near Wmax due to queue buildup at the bottleneck, forcing CUBIC to pause its window growth until the RTT estimator restabilizes itself again.
Wmax is the suspected congestion point, so CUBIC probes cautiously near it and grows fast elsewhere.

questions.explanation: Wmax = suspected congestion point => slow near it, fast far away. CUBIC grows aggressively when far below Wmax to recover lost throughput quickly, then slows near Wmax to avoid triggering another loss.

questions.prompt: In the CUBIC growth curve, the CONCAVE region appears just before W(t) reaches Wmax. What is CUBIC doing there? (Figure: CUBIC — concave region near Wmax (Module 2))

options.text: Slowing its growth as it approaches Wmax, because Wmax is the suspected congestion point and CUBIC wants to land there cautiously.
Probing aggressively above Wmax in case the bottleneck has cleared.
Mimicking Reno's exponential slow-start to ensure backwards compatibility with legacy receivers.
Halving cwnd preemptively whenever it nears Wmax, to avoid the next loss event entirely.

questions.explanation: Concave near Wmax: cautious approach. CUBIC decelerates as cwnd nears the last known congestion point, landing softly to maximise time near the bottleneck capacity without overshooting.

questions.prompt: After cwnd reaches and passes Wmax, the CUBIC curve enters its CONVEX region. What does CUBIC do there? (Figure: CUBIC — convex region past Wmax (Module 2))

options.text: It freezes cwnd at Wmax and waits for an explicit ECN signal to indicate that it may grow further.
It accelerates growth again, probing for new available bandwidth above the previous Wmax, since no new loss event has occurred.
It immediately halves cwnd as a precaution, then enters slow start to re-discover capacity.
It switches to Reno's additive-increase rule (one packet per RTT) and stays in that mode for the lifetime of the connection.

questions.explanation: Above Wmax: accelerate to probe for new capacity. If no loss has happened by the time cwnd exceeds the old Wmax, the bottleneck may have grown; CUBIC ramps up cubically to find the new ceiling.

questions.prompt: CUBIC is said to be RTT-fair while Reno is not. Which property of W(t) = C(t − K)^3 + Wmax produces this? (Figure: CUBIC — RTT-fairness via wall-clock time (Module 2))

options.text: W(t) depends on RTT directly, so connections with shorter RTT grow faster — which gives short-RTT flows priority just as in Reno.
CUBIC negotiates RTT values with the receiver during the handshake, equalizing all flows at the same artificial RTT for the purpose of cwnd updates only.
W(t) depends only on real (wall-clock) time elapsed since the last loss, so two flows with different RTTs grow at the same wall-clock pace and converge to similar throughputs.
CUBIC's W(t) uses RTT^3 in the denominator, exactly cancelling the RTT-dependence of additive increase, leaving Reno's well-known short-RTT bias intact in practice anyway.

questions.explanation: W(t) depends on wall-clock t, not on ACK-driven RTT counts. Two CUBIC flows with very different RTTs grow at the same real-time pace, so they converge to similar throughputs — the property Reno lacks.

questions.prompt: At small cwnd values right after a loss event, CUBIC checks whether Reno's cwnd estimate would be larger than its own. When that happens, CUBIC enters its 'TCP-friendly' mode. What does TCP-friendly mode do? (Figure: CUBIC — TCP-friendly mode at small cwnd (Module 2))

options.text: It runs an independent slow-start episode for the next several RTTs and rebases Wmax to the value at which the most recent loss event was observed, then resumes.
It pauses cwnd updates entirely for one full RTT in order to let coexisting Reno flows catch up before resuming CUBIC's cubic growth function.
It switches to UDP-style send pacing for that RTT, disabling congestion control altogether while the Reno estimate is still ahead of CUBIC's window.
It tracks the Reno-equivalent cwnd estimate so CUBIC's growth never falls below what Reno would have achieved, preserving fairness when sharing a link with Reno flows.

questions.explanation: TCP-friendly = floor cwnd at Reno-equivalent. When sharing a link with Reno, CUBIC ensures its window never sits below what Reno would have used, preventing CUBIC from being unfairly slower than Reno.

questions.prompt: In the CUBIC plot, the inflection point of the cubic curve sits exactly at cwnd = Wmax. Why is that point the inflection? (Figure: CUBIC — inflection point at Wmax (Module 2))

options.text: Because the cubic function W(t) = C(t − K)^3 + Wmax changes its curvature at t = K (where the cube term is zero), and t = K corresponds to cwnd = Wmax by construction.
Because Wmax is the cap value imposed by the receiver, and CUBIC's growth must visibly slow when cwnd hits that cap.
Because at Wmax the connection's RTT estimator transitions from slow start to congestion-avoidance, which mathematically inverts the curvature of any cwnd-vs-time plot.
Because Wmax is the only value at which TCP-friendly mode and CUBIC mode produce the same cwnd; outside of that single point the two modes always diverge at first-order rate.

questions.explanation: Inflection of (t-K)^3 is at t=K, where W=Wmax. The cubic function's curvature flips sign at its root; by design K is chosen so cwnd = Wmax at that instant — the natural mathematical inflection.

questions.prompt: A router has two jobs commonly distinguished as 'forwarding' and 'routing'. Which best characterizes the difference?

options.text: Forwarding is the per-packet act of moving a packet from an input port to the right output port; routing is the cooperative process that builds the forwarding table in the first place.
Forwarding runs in the control plane and routing runs in the data plane, on a different CPU — this is the canonical convention documented in the standard reference for production routers.
Forwarding picks the destination AS; routing only picks the next-hop physical port on a router across the connected subnet.
Forwarding and routing are the same operation, viewed from different timescales by the operator running the network.

questions.explanation: Forwarding = per-packet table lookup; routing = the protocol that BUILDS that table. Forwarding happens millions of times per second on the data plane; routing runs in the background on the control plane to keep the table correct.

questions.prompt: An Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP) is run inside an AS. Which is the most important reason an AS does NOT use BGP for its internal routing?

options.text: BGP cannot run inside one AS at all; the protocol specification forbids same-AS sessions outright in every common implementation.
IGPs converge faster on link cost changes inside the AS and let the operator optimize internal paths by metric, whereas BGP is policy-driven and slow to converge.
IGPs use UDP instead of TCP so they save header bytes on every routing update propagated through the network.
BGP requires hardware TCAMs in every internal router, which most enterprise switches do not have, as is widely deployed across modern Tier-1 networks for predictable inter-domain behaviour.

questions.explanation: IGPs converge fast on link cost; BGP is slow + policy. IGPs (OSPF, IS-IS) optimize the operator's own metric and re-converge in seconds; BGP is designed for inter-AS policy, not for fast internal failover or shortest-path optimization.

questions.prompt: In the graph abstraction used by routing algorithms, nodes are routers and edges are direct router-to-router links with associated costs.
questions.explanation: Routers = nodes, links = edges with cost. The graph abstraction lets routing algorithms ignore physical details (fiber type, link speed) and focus only on topology and link metrics — the inputs Dijkstra and Bellman-Ford actually need.

questions.prompt: What does a link-state algorithm require every router to know BEFORE it runs Dijkstra?

options.text: A complete copy of every other router's forwarding table, refreshed continuously over the routing protocol session in the same domain.
Only its directly attached neighbors and the costs to them; no other state is needed for Dijkstra to terminate correctly.
The cost of every link in the network — typically discovered by flooding link-state advertisements (LSAs) to all other routers.
The MAC address of every host in the AS, refreshed from ARP caches every 30 seconds and propagated to all routers.

questions.explanation: LS = global knowledge: every router needs every link's cost. Each router floods its own neighbor costs as LSAs; once the LSDB is synchronized, every router runs Dijkstra on the same graph to build its own shortest-path tree.

questions.prompt: After Dijkstra terminates at source u, what does u know about destinations across the AS?

options.text: u knows the topology but cannot use it for forwarding decisions; the topology is purely informational.
u knows shortest paths only to its direct neighbors; longer paths are computed lazily on each packet's arrival.
Every other node in the AS knows its own shortest paths to u, but u itself only knows its neighbors.
u knows shortest paths to every other node in the AS — the minimum-cost path and the next hop along it.

questions.explanation: Dijkstra from u => shortest path + next hop to every node. The algorithm doesn't just compute distances — it also yields the next hop along each shortest path, which is what populates the forwarding table.

questions.prompt: In the initialization step of Dijkstra at source u, the label D(v) for a node v that is NOT a direct neighbor of u is set to infinity.
questions.explanation: Init: D(v) = c(u,v) for neighbors, infinity otherwise. Dijkstra starts pessimistic for unknown nodes and only updates D(v) downward when a shorter path through a newly-finalized node is found.

questions.prompt: In the link-state example figure, source u has direct neighbors v, x, w. After initialization, x is added to N' first in iteration 1. Why x and not v or w? (Figure: Link-state example topology (source = u) (Module 3))

options.text: Because x has the lowest initial D value among the nodes not yet in N'.
Because x is alphabetically last among the three candidate neighbors.
Because x has the largest fan-out (most neighbors outside N') and Dijkstra prioritizes high-degree nodes early in convergence.
Because v and w are not direct neighbors of u; only x is connected at all in the initialization stage.

questions.explanation: Lowest-D candidate is added next. Dijkstra's greedy choice: pick the unsettled node with smallest current D, because (with non-negative weights) no shorter path to it can exist via any other unsettled node.

questions.prompt: In the iteration table, after x is added to N', D(w) drops from 5 to 4. Which Dijkstra update produces 4? (Figure: Iteration table — D(w) updated after x joins N' (Module 3))

options.text: max( D(w), c(u,x) + c(x,w) ) = max(5, 1 + 3) = 5, then minus 1 by tiebreaker.
c(u,w) was simply re-measured by the link layer during the algorithm and found to be 4 rather than the originally-reported 5.
The algorithm divided D(w) by the count of x's neighbors after x joined N', producing 4 as a side effect of the iteration.

questions.explanation: Relaxation: D(w) = min(D(w), D(x) + c(x,w)) = min(5, 1+3) = 4. Once x joins N' with D(x)=1, Dijkstra checks each neighbor of x for a shorter path; the path u -> x -> w (cost 4) beats the previous direct path u -> w (cost 5).

questions.prompt: Scenario: in iteration 2 of the same example, two nodes outside N' tie on their D value. Dijkstra needs to add ONE more node to N'. How is the tie broken? (Figure: Dijkstra iteration table (Module 3))

options.text: By geographic proximity to source u — the closer router on the figure is added first — operators rely on this property when designing their routing policies for global reachability.
By the highest-ID node — Dijkstra always prefers the larger numeric router-ID when D values tie among candidate routers in the network.
Arbitrarily — the algorithm picks one of the tied nodes; lecture treats this choice as not affecting the final shortest-path tree from u.
Neither node is added; Dijkstra pauses and waits for an updated LSA to break the tie among the candidates.

questions.explanation: Tie-break is arbitrary; final tree weight is unchanged. Equal D values mean multiple shortest paths exist; Dijkstra is free to pick either — the resulting tree has the same total cost regardless of choice.

questions.prompt: In the figure, all routers in the topology eventually arrive at IDENTICAL shortest-path-tree results for each given source, because they each run the same Dijkstra computation on the same flooded link-state database. (Figure: Link-state example topology (Module 3))
questions.explanation: Same LSDB => same Dijkstra result. Because every router in the area has the identical link-state database after flooding, each independently computes a consistent shortest-path tree from itself — no coordination needed beyond LSA flooding.

questions.prompt: For a network of n routers, Dijkstra's standard implementation has what worst-case computational complexity per source?

options.text: O(log n) — Dijkstra is logarithmic in the number of routers in the network for any heap-based implementation.
O(1) — Dijkstra's running time does not depend on the size of the routing graph in any realistic deployment scenario.
O(n!) — every permutation of routers is enumerated to find the minimum-cost path between source and destination over all n nodes.
O(n^2) — each iteration scans the candidate set linearly, and there are n−1 iterations.

questions.explanation: O(n^2) for simple-array Dijkstra (heap version is O((n+m) log n)). The classic implementation scans the unsettled set linearly each iteration; n iterations x O(n) scan = O(n^2).

questions.prompt: In the Distance Vector figure, each node x updates its DV using a Bellman-Ford-style equation. Which equation does DV use to update Dx(y), x's estimated cost to destination y? (Figure: Distance Vector algorithm — Bellman-Ford updates (Module 3))

options.text: Dx(y) = min over neighbors v of { c(x,v) + Dv(y) }.
Dx(y) = max over neighbors v of { c(x,v) + Dv(y) }, to bound the worst-case path through y.
Dx(y) = c(x,y) only, ignoring all neighbor advertisements — DV considers only direct links.
Dx(y) = sum over neighbors of { c(x,v) + Dv(y) }, normalized by the count of neighbors that have advertised.

questions.explanation: Dx(y) = min over neighbors v of {c(x,v) + Dv(y)}. Bellman-Ford relaxation: x estimates its cost to y as the minimum over each neighbor's advertised cost plus the link cost to that neighbor.

questions.prompt: Consider the DV routing algorithm, each node sends its distance vector to whom?

options.text: To every router in the AS, by flooding the DV throughout the network on every update.
Only to its direct neighbors over the link they share.
Only to the AS border router, which collects all DVs and recomputes the shortest paths centrally.
Only to its providers — DV is suppressed on customer-facing links to control export.

questions.explanation: DV sends only to direct neighbors (not flood). Each node passes its current vector to the routers it shares a link with; over many iterations the information propagates everywhere without anyone needing the global topology.

questions.prompt: Distance Vector is iterative, asynchronous, and distributed: each node updates whenever it gets a new DV from a neighbor.
questions.explanation: DV is iterative, asynchronous, distributed — updates triggered by new neighbor DVs or link changes. Each router runs independently, processes incoming DVs as they arrive, and re-advertises when its own vector changes — no global clock or coordinator.

questions.prompt: In the three-node DV example, x has direct neighbors y (cost 2) and z (cost 7). Initially y and z know only their direct costs. After receiving y's and z's first DVs, x computes Dx(z) using Bellman-Ford. What is Dx(z), and via which next hop? (Figure: DV example — three-node topology x, y, z (Module 3))

options.text: Dx(z) = 9, via the sum c(x,y) + c(x,z) = 2 + 7, since DV always combines both neighbor advertisements when picking a cost estimate.
Dx(z) = 7, via direct link to z (because direct links always win during the first DV iteration regardless of any other update along the path).
Dx(z) = 3, via next hop y (because 2 + Dy(z)=1 yields 3, beating the direct 7).
Dx(z) = ∞, because x's initial DV does not yet contain z and DV cannot compute a cost across two hops in the first iteration.

questions.explanation: Dx(z) = min(c(x,z), c(x,y)+Dy(z)) = min(7, 2+1) = 3. The two-hop path via y (cost 3) beats the direct link (cost 7), so x adopts y as next-hop for destination z.

questions.prompt: In the second iteration of the DV example, node x recomputes Dx(z). Which expression yields the correct converged value of 3? (Figure: DV example — iteration showing x recomputing Dx(z) (Module 3))

options.text: c(x,y) + Dy(y) = 2 + 0 = 2 — DV uses the destination's own self-cost as part of the update, which is zero.
max{ c(x,y) + Dy(z), c(x,z) + Dz(z) } = max(3, 7) = 7, because DV picks the longer of any two candidates.
c(x,z) + Dz(z) = 7 + 0 = 7 — DV never considers indirect paths during the second iteration of the example.

questions.explanation: min{c(x,y)+Dy(z), c(x,z)+Dz(z)} = min(3, 7) = 3. Bellman-Ford picks the minimum across neighbors of (link cost + that neighbor's distance to dest), with the destination's distance to itself being 0.

questions.prompt: In the converged DV tables, once no further updates are produced the nodes stop sending new DV messages until a link cost changes. (Figure: DV example — converged DV tables (Module 3))
questions.explanation: Converged => silence until topology changes. DV stops re-advertising when no node's vector changed; a triggered update fires only when a link cost or neighbor DV produces a new value.

questions.prompt: Suppose during the example, x's link to z (cost 7) becomes unreachable while the y↔z link is still up. After convergence, what does Dx(z) become and through which next hop? (Figure: DV example — second iteration (Module 3))

options.text: Dx(z) = 3, via y (using y's DV entry Dy(z) = 1 plus c(x,y) = 2).
Dx(z) = ∞ — once x's direct link to z fails, x can never reach z, regardless of what its other neighbors advertise about z across the same routing domain.
Dx(z) = 7, because x continues to use the old direct-link cost it had cached until manually purged by an operator command.
Dx(z) = 1, the cost y already advertises to z, copied directly into x's table without any addition of c(x,y) along the path.
