questions.explanation: x falls back to y as next hop (cost 2+1=3). When x's direct link to z dies, its remaining option is via neighbor y — which still has a working path to z — yielding Dx(z) = 3 via next-hop y.

questions.prompt: In the count-to-infinity figure, link y↔x rises sharply in cost (from 4 to 60). Just AFTER y notices the change, y computes a new cost to x of only 6, not 60. Where does the '5' in '1 + 5 = 6' come from? (Figure: Count-to-infinity setup — three-node loop (Module 3))

options.text: From the new direct c(y,x) = 5 measured fresh after the link change settled at the lower value across every router participating in the same routing domain at the same moment.
From z's previously-advertised Dz(x) = 5 (which z computed via y), combined with c(y,z) = 1 — y is unaware that z's path to x goes through y itself.
From the cost c(y,z) = 5 alone, which DV stores in y's table as the cost to z and reuses for x by mistake during the iteration.
From an arbitrary cost cap of 5 that DV applies whenever a route is suspected to be looping through a recently-changed link.

questions.explanation: '5' is z's stale Dz(x) advertised earlier via y. The classic count-to-infinity setup: y doesn't realise z's path to x went through y itself, so y treats z's old advertisement as a valid alternate route and computes 1 + 5 = 6.

questions.prompt: Why does the count-to-infinity scenario take MANY iterations to converge instead of jumping straight to the correct 51? (Figure: Count-to-infinity iterative slow climb (Module 3))

options.text: Because Poison Reverse runs in the background and slows down every advertisement to avoid spurious convergence in DV.
Because DV deliberately throttles updates to one cost increment per second, regardless of the actual change in cost across the network.

Each iteration each node only knows neighbors' last advertised cost plus the direct link cost — neither knows that the cost flowing through the other node now passes through itself, so each round adds only 1 hop to the inflated cost.

Because RIP caps cost increases at 1 per epoch globally, by RFC, because the protocol enforces strict isolation between routing tables in the global system., which the IETF documents as the standard behaviour across all compliant implementations today.

questions.explanation: Each iteration each node only sees the OTHER's last advertised cost. Neither y nor z knows the other's path runs through itself, so each round just adds the direct-link cost — the inflated estimate creeps up by 1 hop per iteration until reaching 'infinity'.

questions.prompt: DV propagates 'good news' (a decrease in link cost) quickly but propagates 'bad news' (a large increase) very slowly — the count-to-infinity problem.
questions.explanation: Good news = decrease propagates in one round; bad news = increase loops slowly. A drop in cost beats every existing alternative immediately; an increase has to wait for every loop-path estimate to also climb past it before convergence.

questions.prompt: Scenario: in a count-to-infinity situation, RIP uses an artificial maximum hop count of 16 to declare 'infinity'. What does this cap actually achieve?

options.text: It signals routers to fail over to BGP when a hop count above 16 is encountered.
It eliminates count-to-infinity entirely for every possible loop topology in any RIP-based network.
It makes RIP converge to the OSPF-best-path on the same topology automatically — multiple RFCs and BCPs prescribe this behaviour for production inter-domain routing systems.
It bounds the worst-case duration of count-to-infinity by capping how many iterations the climb can take before the path is declared unreachable.

questions.explanation: RIP infinity = 16 => bounded count-to-infinity duration. By declaring any path with hop count >= 16 unreachable, RIP guarantees count-to-infinity terminates in finite time — at the cost of disallowing legitimate paths longer than 15 hops.

questions.prompt: In the poison-reverse figure, z's current path to x goes THROUGH y. What does z advertise to y about z's cost to x, and why? (Figure: Poison reverse — z lies to y (Module 3))

options.text: z advertises Dz(x) = ∞ to y; this prevents y from ever choosing to reach x through z, which would create a loop.
z advertises its true Dz(x) to y for fairness across all neighbors.
z stops sending any distance vector to y until the route to x changes — z effectively goes silent on that destination.
z advertises Dz(x) = 0 to y, so y always prefers z for any destination on the network.

questions.explanation: Poison reverse: z lies infinity to its next-hop y. Since z's path to x goes through y, z tells y 'I can't reach x' to prevent y from ever choosing z as a back-up route — which would create a routing loop.

questions.prompt: Scenario: in the poison-reverse figure, suppose z's path to x changes so that z no longer routes via y. What does z's advertisement to y about x look like now? (Figure: Poison reverse semantics (Module 3))

options.text: z keeps advertising Dz(x) = ∞ to y forever, because once poisoning is declared it is permanent for the lifetime of the session.
z advertises its real Dz(x) to y; the poisoning was conditional on z routing through y, so it lifts once z no longer does.
z's advertisement to y is unchanged because poison reverse never reacts to changes in the underlying topology of the AS.
z advertises Dz(x) = 0 to y as a 'cleared' signal, distinct from infinity and from the true cost in the advertisement itself.

questions.explanation: Conditional poisoning: lifts when z no longer routes via y. Poison reverse is per-neighbor and per-destination; once z's path to x stops going through y, the reason to lie disappears and z advertises its true cost again.

questions.prompt: Poison reverse fully eliminates the count-to-infinity problem in all topologies, including loops involving three or more nodes that are not all directly connected.
questions.explanation: PR fixes 2-node loops but NOT longer cycles. Three or more nodes with mutually-reinforcing advertisements can still produce count-to-infinity; poison reverse only protects against the simplest direct-loop case.

questions.prompt: Which statement about OSPF most accurately captures how it differs from RIP?

options.text: OSPF disables Dijkstra and uses Bellman-Ford for the inner loop, but routes only between border routers in each subnet, reflecting how the routing decision flows through a typical commercial-grade daemon at scale.
OSPF is a distance-vector protocol like RIP, but with longer hop-count limits to support modern networks across multiple administrative domains.
OSPF and RIP are equivalent; the names are vendor-specific labels for the same underlying algorithm in different implementations.
OSPF is a link-state protocol — every router floods link-state advertisements (LSAs) to all routers in its area and then independently runs Dijkstra on the shared topology.

questions.explanation: OSPF is link-state: flood LSAs, run Dijkstra locally. Each router publishes its own neighbor info; once every router has the same LSDB, each independently computes its shortest-path tree — no neighbor-to-neighbor DV iterations needed.

questions.prompt: In the hot-potato figure, a router in Dallas has two equally good BGP routes leaving the AS — via San Francisco (IGP cost 9) and New York (IGP cost 10). Which egress does the router pick, and why? (Figure: Hot-potato routing — Dallas chooses between SF and NY egress (Module 3))

options.text: Either at random — hot-potato breaks ties uniformly across equally-good BGP routes when AS-PATH attributes match across the routes.
New York — hot-potato prefers the egress with the HIGHEST IGP cost as a load-balancing mechanism to spread egress traffic.
San Francisco — among equally good BGP routes, hot-potato selects the egress with the lowest IGP cost to push traffic out of the AS sooner.
Neither — hot-potato applies only when BGP costs differ across the candidate egress points — this is the canonical convention documented in the standard reference for production routers.

questions.explanation: Hot-potato: lowest IGP cost wins among equal BGP routes. Push the packet out of the AS as fast as possible (SF, cost 9, vs NY, cost 10) to minimize the carrying cost inside one's own network.

questions.prompt: Scenario: in the figure, what is the consequence of every router in the AS independently making the hot-potato choice for inter-AS traffic? (Figure: Hot-potato routing scenario (Module 3))

options.text: Routers send all traffic on the longest-IGP-cost path to ensure backup links are exercised regularly for failure detection across the AS.
Every packet exits the AS at the same single egress chosen by the BGP best-path selection at the AS border, regardless of internal topology.

All routers send traffic to one central exit chosen by the route reflector — this is the same as a cold-potato policy by definition, as is widely deployed across modern Tier-1 networks for predictable inter-domain behaviour.
Each router minimizes its own AS's internal carrying cost — traffic exits the AS at the closest available egress, which can produce different egress points for different source routers.

questions.explanation: Different sources -> different egress points. Because each router independently picks its closest exit, traffic to the same external destination can leave the AS through different egresses depending on where it originated.

questions.prompt: Hot-potato routing externalizes traffic-carrying cost. From whose perspective is this advantageous, and why might it be controversial?

options.text: Advantageous to the AS that hands traffic off early (low internal cost); controversial because the receiving AS bears the carrying cost across the rest of the path.
Advantageous to the receiving AS because it gets richer traffic data; controversial because the originating AS pays more in transit.

Equally advantageous to both ASes — hot-potato is fully symmetric and well-known to be fair across providers — operators rely on this property when designing their routing policies for global reachability.
Advantageous neither party, but mandated by the IETF for inter-AS traffic of all kinds, leaving operators no choice on the policy.

questions.explanation: Originator dumps cost early; receiver pays it. Hot-potato is advantageous to the AS handing traffic off (low internal carrying cost) but burdens the receiving AS, which must carry the traffic the rest of the way — fair only if both ASes do the same in both directions.

questions.prompt: Looking at the AS-hierarchy figure: a small access ISP that wants to deliver traffic to a distant network outside its providers' tables must rely on which other entity in the picture? (Figure: AS interconnection hierarchy — Tier-1, regional, and access ISPs (Module 4))

options.text: A regional or Tier-1 isp it is a customer of, which forwards the traffic onward.
An IXP route server alone, with no involvement from any provider.
Another access ISP at the same tier as itself, using a settlement-free peering session.
The destination network must connect directly to the access ISP for traffic to be deliverable.

questions.explanation: Small access ISPs reach far destinations via their transit providers. The small ISP doesn't have its own routes to the whole Internet — its provider's BGP table covers everywhere else, and traffic for unknown prefixes is sent upstream.

questions.prompt: In the same hierarchy figure, suppose two Tier-1 ISPs lose their direct peering link. What is the likely effect on a customer of one Tier-1 trying to reach a customer of the other? (Figure: AS interconnection hierarchy (Module 4))

options.text: No effect; Tier-1 ISPs always have multiple direct peerings with every other Tier-1 ISP.
The two halves of the Internet may become partially unreachable between those Tier-1 customer cones until the peering is restored or rerouted.
Traffic gets dropped at the IXP because IXPs cannot connect Tier-1 networks — this is the canonical convention documented in the BGP specification for production routers.
Tier-1 ISPs automatically fall back to using IPv6 in this scenario, restoring connectivity via the v6 plane.

questions.explanation: Direct peering loss between Tier-1s => partial partition. Tier-1s have no transit provider above them, so if direct peers don't reach each other, their customer cones can become mutually unreachable until peering is restored.

questions.prompt: This figure depicts the Internet topology shifting from strictly hierarchical to flatter. What development drove most of this flattening? (Figure: Flattening of the Internet topology (Module 4))

options.text: The Internet now has fewer total ASes than it had a decade ago.
Tier-1 ISPs absorbed all Tier-2 networks via mergers, removing the middle tier.
Large content providers and CDNs deployed at IXPs let networks bypass higher tiers for popular content.
BGP was redesigned to forbid customer-provider relationships and require flat peering.

questions.explanation: CDNs/content providers colocate at IXPs => bypass higher tiers. Popular content (Netflix, YouTube, Akamai) lives close to eyeballs at IXPs, so traffic no longer needs to climb the hierarchy through Tier-1 transit links.

questions.prompt: Looking at the AS business-relationship figure: AS X is a customer of AS Y. In this relationship, who pays whom, and what does X receive? (Figure: Customer / Provider / Peer relationships (Module 4))

options.text: Y pays X; in return X agrees to forward traffic destined for Y's customers along its links.
X and Y both pay the IXP that hosts their interconnection fabric for the duration of the relationship.
Neither pays; the link is settlement-free and serves both parties equally on traffic volume.
X pays Y; in return X receives reachability to every destination Y can reach.

questions.explanation: Customer pays provider for reachability to everywhere. The transit contract: customer pays for upstream access; in return provider announces every route it can reach (default route or full table) to the customer.

questions.prompt: In the figure, two ISPs of similar size establish a settlement-free peering link rather than buying transit. Which is the BEST motivation? (Figure: Customer / Provider / Peer relationships (Module 4))

options.text: To avoid paying their upstream providers for traffic going directly between each other's customers.
To take over the AS-PATH attribute and shorten everyone's routes uniformly across the Internet.
To merge their two ASes into one for administrative simplicity in a single global table.
To allow both ASes to begin charging Tier-1 ISPs for transit service over time.

questions.explanation: Peering avoids paying transit on traffic to each other's customers. Two similar-size ISPs save money by exchanging customer-bound traffic directly; previously they each paid upstream providers to carry it.

questions.prompt: Scenario: AS X learns the SAME prefix from a customer AS, a peer AS, and a provider AS at the same moment. Which path will X most likely install for forwarding, and why? (Figure: Customer / Provider / Peer relationships (Module 4))

options.text: The peer-learned route, because peers are usually closer in the topology and have lower AS-PATH lengths than providers.
The customer-learned route, because forwarding via customer earns X revenue while the other two cost X money or provide no revenue.
The provider-learned route, because providers always advertise the most stable and well-vetted paths to a destination.
Whichever has the shortest AS-PATH; relationship type plays no role in this decision, as is widely deployed across modern Tier-1 networks for predictable inter-domain behaviour.

questions.explanation: Customer route is preferred => revenue. Among same-prefix routes, exporting via a customer earns money; via a provider costs money; via a peer is settlement-free — BGP best-path is overridden by relationship preference (via LocalPref).

questions.prompt: In a customer-provider relationship, the customer can use the provider's link to reach every destination the provider knows about.
questions.explanation: Customer = full transit reachability. The customer-provider contract grants the customer access to everything the provider can reach, which is typically the full Internet or a default route.

questions.prompt: From the import/export policy figure: AS X learns a route to prefix p from one of its CUSTOMERS. To which neighbors will X export p, in the standard Gao–Rexford convention? (Figure: Import/export policy decisions by relationship (Module 4))

options.text: Only to its customers; X does not announce customer routes to peers or providers.
Only to its providers; customer routes are intended to climb the hierarchy.
To all neighbors — customers, peers, and providers — to maximize reachability for p across the Internet.
Only to its peers, never to its customers, since peers reciprocate the announcement.

questions.explanation: Customer routes are exported to EVERYONE. The customer pays for transit, so the provider announces the customer's prefixes everywhere (other customers, peers, providers) to maximize traffic earned for that customer.

questions.prompt: From the same figure: AS X learns a route to prefix p from one of its PROVIDERS. Whom does X announce p to, under standard policy? (Figure: Import/export policy decisions by relationship (Module 4))

options.text: All neighbors of X — providers, peers, and customers alike — to maximize reachability.
Providers and peers only — never customers, since customers are paying for transit.
Peers only — peering reciprocity demands re-announcement to peers but not customers.
Customers only.

questions.explanation: Provider routes are exported only to customers. Re-announcing a provider's route to peers or other providers would cost money to carry that traffic without any incoming revenue — so Gao-Rexford restricts to customer-only.

questions.prompt: Why does an AS typically WITHHOLD a route learned from a provider when re-announcing to its peers and other providers?

options.text: Because the AS would carry traffic for other networks while paying its own provider for that traffic, with no incoming revenue to offset it.
Because BGP forbids re-advertising any route ever learned over eBGP — operators rely on this property when designing their routing policies for global reachability.
Because peer and provider routers refuse to accept routes longer than 3 AS hops along the path.
Because MED attributes from providers cannot be re-exported by routing software.

questions.explanation: Re-announcing provider route would cost money without revenue. The AS would be carrying transit traffic for non-customers — paying its own provider to receive it and earning nothing — a clear losing position.

questions.prompt: BGP is a path-vector protocol rather than a link-state protocol like OSPF. Which characteristic best explains why path-vector was preferred at AS scale?

options.text: Path-vector protocols converge faster than link-state in every topology, regardless of size.
Path-vector lets each AS apply its own policy decisions and hide internal topology from other ASes, while still carrying enough information to avoid loops.
Path-vector consumes less memory than link-state databases by storing only destination addresses, without any path information.
Path-vector is the only design that supports IPv6 in the wider Internet today across every router participating in the same routing domain at the same moment.

questions.explanation: Path-vector hides topology + supports policy. BGP carries an AS-PATH (loop detection, prefix length, route preference) without revealing internal links; each AS independently applies its own export/import policies.

questions.prompt: In the BGP-basics figure, the session between border router 3a (in AS3) and border router 1c (in AS1) is best classified as which? (Figure: BGP topology — AS1, AS2, AS3 with border routers (Module 4))

options.text: iBGP, because both endpoints are border routers and BGP between border routers is internal.
An IGP adjacency such as OSPF; only inside-AS sessions use BGP.
eBGP, because the two routers belong to different ASes.
A route-server multilateral session, since they are at different ASes.

questions.explanation: eBGP = between ASes; iBGP = same AS. Border routers 3a and 1c are in different ASes (3 and 1), so the session between them is external BGP by definition, regardless of router role.

questions.prompt: Looking at the figure, an eBGP session between 3a and 1c suddenly goes down. What is the immediate effect on the BGP routes those two routers had been exchanging? (Figure: BGP topology — AS1, AS2, AS3 (Module 4))

options.text: Nothing; routes persist in the RIB until manually deleted by an operator on each side.
The IXP automatically takes over the session and continues forwarding announcements on behalf of both sides.
Only AS1 withdraws routes; AS3 holds onto its learned entries until it receives a new explicit withdraw.
Both routers withdraw the routes learned from each other and propagate the withdrawal to their iBGP and eBGP neighbors.

questions.explanation: Both sides withdraw and propagate. When an eBGP session drops, both routers invalidate everything they learned from each other; that triggers iBGP withdrawals internally and eBGP withdrawals to other peers.

questions.prompt: A BGP UPDATE message can contain which of the following content categories?

options.text: Both announcements (newly available routes) and withdrawals (no-longer-valid routes) in the same message.
Only withdrawals; announcements use a different message type, because BGP enforces strict isolation between AS routing tables in the global routing system.
Only announcements of newly available routes; withdrawals require a separate WITHDRAW message type.
Neither announcements nor withdrawals; UPDATE messages carry only KEEPALIVE counters between peers.

questions.explanation: UPDATE = announcements + withdrawals in one message. A BGP UPDATE encodes both new reachability and routes that should be removed in a single packet — there's no separate WITHDRAW message type.

questions.prompt: In the iBGP/eBGP figure: border router the left router in AS2 learns a route to prefix p via eBGP from AS1. How does the right internal router in AS2 learn about p? (Figure: iBGP/eBGP topology — border routers and internal routers within one AS (Module 4))

options.text: It learns p directly over eBGP from AS1's border router.
It receives p inside AS2 via an iBGP session from the left router.
It learns p via an IGP such as OSPF that carries external BGP routes alongside internal ones.
It cannot learn about this prefix at all.

questions.explanation: Internal routers learn external prefixes via iBGP. Border router receives p over eBGP from outside; it then redistributes p via iBGP to internal routers so they know how to forward toward that prefix.

questions.prompt: From the iBGP full-mesh figure: an AS currently has 5 BGP-speaking routers connected in a full mesh of iBGP sessions. The operator adds a 6th BGP router. How many NEW iBGP sessions are required? (Figure: iBGP full-mesh requirement (Module 4))

options.text: 1 new session — just one connection to the nearest existing router suffices.
No new sessions — iBGP scales automatically because each router gossips to its IGP-discovered neighbors.
6 new sessions — one extra for redundancy with each existing router.
5 new sessions — one to each of the existing routers, to preserve the full mesh.

questions.explanation: n -> n+1 routers => +n new iBGP sessions. Full mesh on n routers has n(n-1)/2 sessions; adding one more requires a session to each of the existing n routers — for n=5, that's 5 new sessions.

questions.prompt: Looking at the BGP decision-pipeline figure, which order describes how an incoming route progresses through a BGP router? (Figure: BGP route-handling pipeline (Module 4))

options.text: Export policy → decision process → install in FIB → import policy.
Import policy → decision process → install in forwarding table → export policy.
Install in FIB → decision process → import policy → export policy.
Decision process → import policy → export policy → install in FIB.

questions.explanation: Import -> decision process -> install in FIB -> export. Incoming routes are first filtered by import policy, then the BGP decision process picks the best path, then it's installed in the forwarding table, and finally export policy decides what to re-advertise.

questions.prompt: Scenario: AS X learns the same prefix p via three eBGP neighbors. The three routes have LocalPref values 100, 200, 100 respectively. Which route does BGP install, and why? (Figure: BGP decision attributes — LocalPref, AS-PATH, MED (Module 4))

options.text: Whichever route arrived first in time, since LocalPref is only a tiebreaker for the rare equal-AS-PATH case.
The route with LocalPref 100 that has the shortest AS-PATH, because LocalPref ties go to AS-PATH.
The route with LocalPref 200, because BGP's first decision step (after policy) prefers the highest LocalPref value.
The route with LocalPref 100 from the LEFT-most neighbor, because BGP always prefers the lowest router-ID.

questions.explanation: Highest LocalPref wins (step 1 of decision). LocalPref is the first tie-breaker after import policy: 200 beats 100, and AS-PATH length is only consulted if LocalPref is tied.

questions.prompt: Scenario from the LocalPref figure: AS B learns the same prefix via neighbor A AND via neighbor C. B wants traffic to leave through A. What is the simplest policy knob B configures? (Figure: Using LocalPref to control outbound exit choice (Module 4))

options.text: B advertises a longer AS-PATH out to neighbor C by AS-path prepending its own ASN multiple times.
B disables its iBGP sessions to internal routers so they all default to using A.
B lowers the MED it advertises TO A, hoping A will prefer that route in its own decision process.
B sets a higher LocalPref on the route received from A than on the route received from C.

questions.explanation: Higher LocalPref on A's route = prefer A as exit. LocalPref is the standard knob for outbound traffic engineering: set it higher on the route from A and BGP will pick A's route over C's regardless of AS-PATH length.

questions.prompt: Scenario from the MED figure: AS B has two parallel links to AS A, through routers R1 and R2. B advertises the same prefix on both links and tags the R1 advertisement with a LOWER MED. Where does A's traffic for that prefix ENTER AS B? (Figure: MED influences inbound traffic entry point (Module 4))

options.text: Through R1, because A's BGP decision process prefers the lower MED among routes equal in earlier steps.
Through R2, because higher MED is preferred at the receiver side of BGP best-path selection.
Equally split across R1 and R2, since MED is only a hint and not used for hard routing decisions.
Through neither; A discards both routes because conflicting MED values are treated as a misconfiguration.

questions.explanation: Lower MED at R1 => A enters via R1. MED is compared at the receiver among routes from the SAME AS; lower MED wins, so A's BGP decision process picks R1 as the entry into AS B.

questions.prompt: MED is named 'Multi-Exit Discriminator', yet operators use it to influence INBOUND traffic. What does the 'exit' in the name actually refer to?

options.text: The exit interface on a downstream IXP route-server, not on any AS, reflecting how the routing decision flows through a typical commercial-grade BGP daemon at scale.
The exit point of the receiving AS — i.e., MED helps the neighbor decide which of its own exit links to use when sending traffic toward you.
The exit code of the BGP daemon when it crashes due to malformed MED values from peers.
The exit from slow-start mode in the underlying TCP connection that carries the BGP session.

questions.explanation: 'Exit' = exit of the RECEIVING AS. MED tells your neighbor which of THEIR exits toward you to prefer — you're influencing inbound traffic by hinting which of your neighbor's egress links should carry it.

questions.prompt: BGP includes a tie-breaker preferring 'eBGP-learned over iBGP-learned'. What is the intuition behind preferring eBGP routes when other attributes are equal?

options.text: eBGP routes are signed cryptographically and iBGP routes are not — this is the canonical convention documented in the BGP specification for production routers.
eBGP routes always have shorter AS-PATH attributes than iBGP routes by construction.
eBGP routes typically reach the destination through fewer internal hops, sending traffic out of the AS sooner ('hot-potato' routing).
eBGP routes consume less memory in the RIB than iBGP routes, conserving router resources.

questions.explanation: eBGP > iBGP => hot-potato. Among otherwise-equal routes, prefer the one learned via eBGP because that exits the AS sooner, minimizing the carrying cost inside your own network.

questions.prompt: In the BGP decision process, LocalPref is evaluated AFTER AS-PATH length.
questions.explanation: LocalPref is evaluated FIRST, before AS-PATH. The decision order: LocalPref -> AS-PATH length -> origin -> MED -> eBGP/iBGP -> IGP cost -> router-ID. LocalPref dominates by design so policy beats path length.

questions.prompt: BGP convergence after a route withdrawal can take many seconds or longer. What is the primary REASON convergence is slow at this scale?

options.text: BGP UPDATEs are sent over UDP, which often loses messages and triggers retransmission storms.

BGP uses Spanning Tree Protocol internally, which has long stabilization timers, as is widely deployed across modern Tier-1 networks for predictable inter-domain behaviour. — operators rely on this property when designing their routing policies for global reachability.
The AS-PATH attribute is cryptographically signed, and verification takes seconds per hop.
Each affected AS must process the withdrawal, re-run its decision process, possibly explore alternative AS-PATHs, and propagate updates onward — and many ASes do this in series.

questions.explanation: Convergence is serial across ASes + alternative path exploration. Each AS must process the withdrawal, potentially try every alternative AS-PATH (path exploration), then propagate onward — so wall-clock time scales with the diameter of the affected portion.

questions.prompt: Scenario: a small AS misconfigures its export policy and accidentally re-announces ALL routes from one provider out to another. What is the typical worst-case effect on the wider Internet?

options.text: A route leak in which the misconfigured AS attracts large amounts of transit traffic, overloads its links, and may cause global reachability outages until upstreams filter the leak.
Nothing visible beyond the misconfiguring AS, since BGP filters prevent route propagation to non-customers automatically by default in modern implementations.
The misconfigured routes are silently dropped by every other AS because BGP requires cryptographic origin authentication for every announcement.

The misconfigured AS automatically loses its IP allocation from its RIR within minutes of the leak being detected by RPKI infrastructure across every router participating in the same routing domain at the same moment.

questions.explanation: Route leak: AS becomes unintended transit => attracts traffic. When provider-learned routes are re-exported to other providers/peers, traffic floods toward the leaking AS, overloading its links until upstreams filter the bogus announcements.

questions.prompt: BGP has built-in cryptographic authentication that prevents any AS from forging the origin of a prefix.
questions.explanation: BGP has no built-in origin authentication. Any AS can announce any prefix; preventing forgery requires layering RPKI/BGPsec on top — neither is universally deployed.

questions.prompt: From the IXP figure: an IXP is best described as which of the following? (Figure: IXP topology — multiple ASes interconnected via shared switching fabric (Module 4))

options.text: A protocol layered on top of BGP that replaces BGP for inter-AS routing.
A physical (often switched-Ethernet) infrastructure where many networks interconnect to exchange traffic locally rather than via transit.
A Tier-1 ISP that resells transit to all other tiers, because BGP enforces strict isolation between AS routing tables in the global routing system.
An authoritative registry that allocates ASNs and IP prefixes to networks.

questions.explanation: IXP = shared Layer-2 fabric for many ASes. Physically it's a switch (or set of switches) at one location where many networks plug in and run BGP sessions across that fabric to exchange traffic without using a transit provider.

questions.prompt: Scenario: a small ISP joins an IXP that has 100 other members. Without a route server, the ISP must set up how many BGP sessions to peer with all the others?

options.text: 1 session in total, into the shared IXP fabric, which broadcasts routes to everyone.
Exactly 10 sessions, because IXPs cap bilateral peerings at 10 per member for fairness.
Up to 100 separate bilateral BGP sessions — one with each member it wants to peer with.
Zero sessions — the IXP relays all routes automatically without BGP.

questions.explanation: 100 peers without route server => up to 100 bilateral sessions. Each pair-wise peering needs its own BGP session; full bilateral peering at a 100-member IXP means up to 100 sessions per member.

questions.prompt: Why does IXP-based peering tend to FLATTEN the Internet hierarchy?

options.text: IXPs are forbidden from connecting to Tier-1 networks, so all peering must happen below the top of the hierarchy and the hierarchy collapses.
IXPs internally use a different routing protocol than BGP, which is incompatible with hierarchical AS relationships.

IXPs use UDP rather than TCP, which avoids the layering required by Tier-1 ISPs, which the IETF documents as the standard behaviour across all compliant BGP implementations today. — multiple RFCs and BCPs prescribe this behaviour for production inter-domain routing systems.
Many networks exchange large volumes of traffic directly through the IXP without paying a higher-tier provider to carry that traffic, eroding the strict customer/provider hierarchy.

questions.explanation: Direct exchange at IXPs => no upstream needed for that traffic. Networks bypass climbing to Tier-1s for the volumes they exchange at the IXP, eroding the strict customer/provider hierarchy.

questions.prompt: Two ISPs of similar size that peer at an IXP typically do so to AVOID paying a common upstream provider for the traffic between them.
questions.explanation: Peering at IXP avoids paying upstream for that traffic. Same logic as private peering, just done at shared infrastructure — both ASes save the per-bit transit cost they would otherwise pay providers.

questions.prompt: A participant AS uses the route server for MULTILATERAL peering at an IXP. How many BGP sessions does that AS open? (Figure: IXP with a route server (multilateral peering) (Module 4))

options.text: One BGP session — to the route server itself — which exchanges routes with many other participants on its behalf.
One BGP session per other participant, just as in bilateral peering, except the sessions are tunneled through the route server.
Zero sessions; the route server installs forwarding rules directly into each member router via OpenFlow.
One session per IPv4 prefix the AS announces, for granular policy control.

questions.explanation: Route server = ONE session multiplexed to many peers. Instead of N bilateral sessions, a member opens a single BGP session to the route server, which relays routes to/from all other participants per per-pair filters.

questions.prompt: AS X announces prefix p to the route server. Walk through what the route server does next. (Figure: Route server processing — per-participant RIBs and master RIB (Module 4))

options.text: Stores p in its master RIB, applies a single global filter, and re-announces p to every member immediately.

Stores p in X's per-participant RIB, applies X's import filter, adds it to the master RIB, then for each potential recipient applies X's export filter (does X want Z to see p?) before announcing to Z.

Drops p because route servers only forward established session traffic, not new advertisements, reflecting how the routing decision flows through a typical commercial-grade BGP daemon at scale. — this is the canonical convention documented in the BGP specification for production routers.
Installs p as a hardware flow rule in every member's switch via the IXP's SDN controller and bypasses BGP entirely.

questions.explanation: RIB-In -> import filter -> master RIB -> per-recipient export filter -> RIB-Out. Each member's announcements are stored separately and per-recipient export filters decide who actually sees what — preserving bilateral policy on top of multilateral session.

questions.prompt: AS X announces p to the route server, but X has configured an export filter blocking AS Z. What does AS Z see? (Figure: Route server export filters per participant pair (Module 4))

options.text: Z sees p with the route server as the next hop, since route servers always re-announce regardless of filters.
Z sees p but with the AS-PATH replaced by 'AS-RouteServer', allowing routing but logging the suppressed origin.
Z receives nothing for p; the route server respects X's per-recipient export filter and withholds p from Z.
Z sees p only after manually downloading the IXP's policy table via an out-of-band API.

questions.explanation: Export filter respects per-recipient policy => Z gets nothing. Even though the route server has p in its RIB, X's filter blocks announcement to Z; route servers honor each member's policy as if it were direct bilateral peering.

questions.prompt: A route server, by design, is 'transparent' in the AS-PATH — i.e., the route server's ASN does NOT appear in any AS-PATH it relays. Why?

options.text: Because route servers run a custom transport protocol that strips the AS-PATH attribute during relay, as is widely deployed across modern Tier-1 networks for predictable inter-domain behaviour. — operators rely on this property when designing their routing policies for global reachability.
Because route servers operate at Layer 2 and have no Layer-3 visibility into the BGP messages they relay.
Because IANA has never allocated an ASN to any IXP route server, and BGP requires a real ASN to be inserted.

Because if the route server's ASN were inserted, every multilateral session would falsely lengthen AS-PATHs by 1, biasing best-path selection against route-server routes — so the route server simply passes through the original AS-path unchanged.

questions.explanation: Inserting RS ASN would inflate AS-PATH by 1 => bias best-path. Best-path selection prefers shorter AS-PATH; if the RS added itself, multilateral routes would always look longer than bilateral and lose comparisons unfairly.

questions.prompt: Looking at the router-components figure, where does a router's CONTROL plane functionality (running routing protocols, computing the forwarding table) live, and where does the DATA plane live? (Figure: Router components — input ports, switching fabric, output ports, routing processor (Module 5))

options.text: Control plane runs in software on the routing processor; data plane runs in hardware at the input/output ports and switching fabric.
Both control and data planes run on dedicated ASICs at the line cards, with no software involvement at the routing processor.
Control plane runs at output ports; data plane runs at input ports — they are physically separate but co-located.
Both planes run in the central CPU; modern routers have no specialized hardware for either function.

questions.explanation: Control = SW on route processor; data = HW on line cards/fabric. The control plane computes the FIB (slowly, in software); the data plane uses that FIB to forward packets at line rate in custom ASICs.

questions.prompt: In the input-port pipeline figure, after physical termination and data-link decapsulation, what is the most important function performed before the packet crosses the switching fabric? (Figure: Router input-port pipeline (Module 5))

options.text: Recomputing every TCP checksum carried in segments transiting the router.
Looking up the destination prefix in the forwarding table to choose an outgoing port.
Running an OSPF update on every received packet to update link-state advertisements.
Buffering all packets at the input port until a scheduling round picks them up later.

questions.explanation: Input-port pipeline: physical -> link-decap -> FIB lookup. The forwarding decision (which output port?) is the input port's main job; everything else is preparation or follow-through.

questions.prompt: Looking at the output-port pipeline figure: which task is performed at the OUTPUT port that is NOT also performed at the input port? (Figure: Output-port pipeline (Module 5))

options.text: Decrementing the TTL field in the IP header — TTL decrement is an output-port-only task.
Looking up the destination prefix in the forwarding table — this is repeated at output for verification.
Queueing packets for transmission and scheduling which queued packet to send next.
Decoding the application-layer URL from HTTP payloads for content-aware forwarding.

questions.explanation: Output port adds queueing + scheduling. The output port buffers packets contending for the same outgoing link and chooses which to send next (FIFO, WFQ, DRR,...) — the input port has no scheduling role.

questions.prompt: In the figure, the routing processor's job is to run routing protocols and program the FIB (Forwarding Information Base) used by the data plane; it does not normally see every packet. (Figure: Routing processor and control-plane interaction (Module 5))
questions.explanation: Route processor programs the FIB; it doesn't touch every packet. Routing protocols (OSPF, BGP) and FIB updates run in software on the route processor; the per-packet data plane runs on the line cards.

questions.prompt: The router-architecture figure separates 'fast-path' (per-packet hardware) tasks from 'slow-path' or 'less time-sensitive' tasks. Which task belongs to the SLOW path? (Figure: Router architecture — separation of header validation, lookup, switching, scheduling (Module 5))

options.text: Scheduling of queued packets for transmission on the outgoing link.
Longest-prefix match in the FIB on every incoming packet.
Movement of the packet across the switching fabric to the chosen output port.
TTL decrement and header checksum recomputation as part of header validation.

questions.explanation: Header validation = less time-sensitive path. TTL decrement, checksum, options — these are 'slow-path' compared to the per-packet fast path of fabric switching and LPM lookup; they can be handled with more time per packet.

questions.prompt: In a modern router, the data plane operates at nanosecond timescales in hardware, while the control plane operates at second/millisecond timescales in software.
questions.explanation: Data plane = ns (HW); control plane = ms-to-s (SW). Per-packet decisions must complete in nanoseconds to sustain line rate; routing protocols and FIB recomputation have orders of magnitude more headroom.

questions.prompt: In the shared-memory switching figure, what limits the router's per-packet throughput? (Figure: Switching via shared memory (Module 5))

options.text: The memory's read/write bandwidth — every packet is read in then written out, so the memory cycles are the bottleneck.
The number of input ports on the router — more ports automatically speed up memory access in shared-memory designs.
The CPU clock rate alone, regardless of the memory subsystem in any conventional shared-memory router.
The TCP window size of each transiting flow during burst arrivals on the line cards.

questions.explanation: Shared memory: 2 mem cycles per packet => memory bandwidth caps. Every packet is written into shared memory then read out, so the memory's bandwidth and access latency directly limit throughput.

questions.prompt: In the shared-bus switching figure, what limits the router's per-packet throughput? (Figure: Switching via shared bus (Module 5))

options.text: The forwarding-table size; bigger tables slow down the bus arbitration logic.
The bus itself — only one packet can be on the bus at any moment, so the bus bandwidth caps the router.
The number of output queues — the bus is unaffected by traffic intensity even at saturation.
The clock skew between input and output ports across the bus — this is the canonical convention documented in the standard reference for production routers.

questions.explanation: Shared bus: only one packet at a time on the bus => bus bandwidth caps. The bus serializes all transfers; even with many ports, total throughput can't exceed bus bandwidth.

questions.prompt: In the crossbar switching figure for an N×N fabric, how many buses (input + output) are needed? (Figure: Crossbar switching fabric — 2N buses with crosspoints (Module 5))

options.text: N^2 buses, one per crosspoint, to ensure non-blocking switching at any traffic load.
N buses total — shared between input and output in a single direction.
2N buses — N horizontal input buses and N vertical output buses connected at crosspoints.
log2(N) buses — modern crossbars use a binary fan-out for scalability.

questions.explanation: Crossbar = 2N buses (N input + N output). Each input has a horizontal bus, each output has a vertical bus, and crosspoints connect them — enabling N independent simultaneous transfers if no two share an output.

questions.prompt: Scenario in the crossbar figure: input A wants to send a packet to output Y at the same time as input B wants to send to output X. Can both transfers happen in the same cell time? (Figure: Crossbar fabric — parallel non-conflicting transfers (Module 5))

options.text: No — crossbar fabrics serialize transfers when more than one input is active.
No — a crossbar can only switch one packet per cell time, regardless of inputs and outputs.
Yes, but only if A and B are physically adjacent input ports on the line card chassis.
Yes — both transfers use disjoint input and output buses, so the crossbar can switch them in parallel.

questions.explanation: Disjoint input AND output => parallel transfers in one cell time. A crossbar's strength: NxN independent crosspoints let multiple non-conflicting transfers happen simultaneously, scaling well with port count.

questions.prompt: Scenario in the same crossbar figure: TWO inputs (A and B) both want to send to the SAME output Y in the same cell time. What happens? (Figure: Crossbar fabric — output contention (Module 5))

options.text: Only one of the two transfers can proceed in that cell time; the other waits in its input queue, exposing the head-of-line blocking problem the next lesson addresses.
Both packets are sent simultaneously through the same output bus by time-multiplexing the bits inside a single cell time.
The crossbar merges the two packets into a combined frame and forwards the result to Y, as is widely deployed across modern Tier-1 networks for predictable inter-domain behaviour.
The output port itself drops both packets and triggers an ICMP source-quench back to A and B.

questions.explanation: Same output => one wins, other waits => head-of-line blocking. When two inputs target the same output, only one transfer proceeds; the loser is stuck at the head of its input queue, blocking even unrelated packets behind it (HoL).

questions.prompt: The figure lists three challenges modern routers must solve at high speed. Which is NOT one of those challenges?

options.text: Performing longest-prefix matching at line rate on every arriving packet.
Cryptographically signing every BGP route the router advertises onward.
Storing very large forwarding tables compactly so memory access stays cheap.
Moving each packet from input to output port within nanoseconds at full link rate.

questions.explanation: BGP signing happens at the control plane (and is rare in practice). Cryptographic origin authentication (RPKI/BGPsec) is a control-plane task, not a per-packet data-plane challenge that routers must solve at line rate.

questions.prompt: As link speeds grow (e.g., to 100+ Gbps), the per-packet time budget shrinks — making efficient lookup data structures more important, not less.
questions.explanation: Faster links => shorter per-packet time => harder lookups. At 100 Gbps with small packets, a router has nanoseconds per packet — making any inefficient lookup data structure a bottleneck.

questions.prompt: Why does the Internet's forwarding table use LONGEST-PREFIX match instead of exact match against a destination address?

options.text: Longest-prefix match is faster than exact match because hardware tries always converge in O(1) regardless of input length.
Exact match is patented by major router vendors and cannot be used in software-defined networks at scale.
The number of hosts is far too large for per-destination entries, so destinations are grouped into prefixes; one address may match many prefixes, and the longest (most specific) wins.
Exact match is only used for IPv6, while longest-prefix match is reserved for IPv4 forwarding lookups — operators rely on this property when designing their routing policies for global reachability.

questions.explanation: LPM = group by prefix, most-specific wins. Storing per-host entries is infeasible; CIDR aggregates addresses into prefixes, and overlapping prefixes are resolved by picking the longest (most-specific) match.

questions.prompt: Scenario in the LPM figure: a destination IP matches both prefix 10.0.0.0/8 and prefix 10.1.2.0/24. Which entry does the router use to forward, and why?

options.text: Neither — overlapping prefixes are detected as a misconfiguration and packets are dropped at the input.
10.0.0.0/8 — shorter prefixes are preferred because they aggregate more destinations.
Both — the router duplicates the packet and sends one copy out of each next hop in parallel.
10.1.2.0/24 — among matches, the longest (most-specific) prefix wins.

questions.explanation: Longest = most specific = chosen. Among matching prefixes 10.0/8 and 10.1.2/24, the /24 represents tighter routing information (a more specific destination) and takes precedence.

questions.prompt: In the unibit-trie figure, each node has at most how many children, and what determines which child is taken? (Figure: Unibit trie — binary branching per bit (Module 5))

options.text: Two children (a 0-pointer and a 1-pointer); each step inspects one more bit of the address from left to right.
Four children — modern tries use a 2-bit stride per node even when labeled 'unibit'.
One child — unibit tries are linear chains by definition in every implementation.
Variable, depending on the number of prefixes in the database for that subtrie.

questions.explanation: Two children per node (0-pointer, 1-pointer); one bit consumed per step. The unibit trie binary-searches the prefix space one address bit at a time, descending until no further match exists.

questions.prompt: In the figure, node P9 represents a compressed one-way branch stored as a short string of bits. Why does the trie compress these chains rather than store one node per bit? (Figure: Unibit trie — compressed branch P9 (Module 5))

options.text: Compressed nodes let the trie support IPv6 addresses without rebuilding the lookup engine.
Long chains of single-child nodes waste memory — replacing them with a single labeled string saves space without changing lookup correctness.
Compression is required by the IETF's CIDR specification for routers used in the global Internet.
Without compression, the trie would have O(n!) memory at scale across every router participating in the same routing domain at the same moment.

questions.explanation: Chains of single-child nodes wasted memory => collapse into label. Compressed unibit tries store long chains as a single labeled string (e.g., '01101'), preserving correctness while cutting node count dramatically.

questions.prompt: Scenario from the unibit-trie figure: a packet's destination begins with the bits 101.... Starting from the root, what pointer sequence does the lookup follow? (Figure: Unibit trie — traversal of bits 101 (Module 5))

options.text: Both pointers at each level in parallel; the trie performs a hardware-level AND of the results.
0-pointer (left), 1-pointer (right), 0-pointer (left) — bits read right-to-left.
1-pointer (right), 0-pointer (left), 1-pointer (right) — one step per bit.
Only the rightmost pointer at every level — unibit tries collapse all left subtrees at lookup time.

questions.explanation: Bits 101 => right-left-right pointers (one step per bit). Each bit picks 0-pointer or 1-pointer; trie walks left for 0, right for 1, consuming one bit per level.

questions.prompt: In a multibit trie with stride k, how many children does each non-leaf node have, and what does that buy you?

options.text: k^2 children — each pair of bits gets its own subtree for variable-stride flexibility.
2k children — strides are added linearly, doubling lookup throughput per stride increment.
k children — one per bit examined, which is the same as a unibit trie with batch reads.
2^k children per node, examining k bits per step — fewer total memory accesses per lookup at the cost of larger nodes.

questions.explanation: Stride k => 2^k children, k bits per step. A multibit trie examines k bits at a time (a 'stride'), so each node has 2^k children — fewer total memory accesses, larger nodes.

questions.prompt: In a fixed-stride trie with stride k, which prefix lengths fit DIRECTLY without expansion, and what is done about the rest? (Figure: Prefix expansion — extending short prefixes to the next stride boundary (Module 5))

options.text: Only prefix lengths that are multiples of k fit directly. Shorter or non-multiple prefixes are expanded — replicated across all bit-patterns that extend them to the next multiple of k.
All prefix lengths fit directly; expansion is purely an optional optimization not required by the trie.
Only prefix lengths shorter than k fit directly; longer ones are split into k-bit chunks separately.
Only prefix lengths equal to 32 fit directly in IPv4; everything else is rounded up to 32 bits, because the protocol enforces strict isolation between routing tables in the global system.

questions.explanation: Multiples of k fit; others expanded to next stride boundary. A length-5 prefix in a stride-3 trie spans 12/3 levels; expansion replicates it across all bit-patterns that extend it to the next stride boundary (length 6).

questions.prompt: Scenario: during prefix expansion with stride 3, prefix P3 = 11001* (length 5) expands to extend up to a 6-bit pattern. What is the expanded set? (Figure: Prefix expansion with collision (Module 5))

options.text: 11001* alone — no actual expansion needed because length 5 is already 'close enough' to a stride multiple in modern routers.
110010* and 110011* — two entries that together cover all 1-bit extensions of P3 to the next multiple of 3.
11001000* through 11001111* — 8 entries, expanding all 3 trailing bits to the next stride boundary.
1100* and 1101* — expansion shrinks the prefix to the previous stride boundary, then re-splits it.

questions.explanation: P3=11001* expanded => {110010*, 110011*}. Stride 3 means we descend in 3-bit chunks (lengths 0/3/6/9/...); a length-5 prefix needs one more bit, so it becomes both 110010* and 110011*.

questions.prompt: Scenario: an expanded prefix entry happens to collide with a more-specific (longer) prefix already in the database. What does the algorithm do? (Figure: Prefix expansion — collision with longer prefix (Module 5))

options.text: It overwrites the more-specific prefix with the expanded one (most-recent-wins).
It keeps both, and lookup picks one at random at runtime, which the IETF documents as the standard behaviour across all compliant implementations today.
It drops the expanded (less-specific) entry, keeping the more-specific one — longest-prefix match wins.
It rebuilds the trie with a larger stride to avoid the collision in the future.

questions.explanation: Longest-prefix wins => drop expanded duplicate. If expansion would overwrite a more-specific prefix already present, the more-specific entry is preserved (LPM rule); the expanded entry is dropped at that bit pattern.

questions.prompt: Prefix expansion lets fixed-stride multibit tries support prefixes that are NOT exact multiples of the stride length.
questions.explanation: Expansion fits non-multiple lengths into fixed-stride tries. Without expansion, only prefix lengths that are exact multiples of k could be stored; expansion makes any prefix length representable at the cost of replication.

questions.prompt: In the fixed-stride trie figure (the famous Practice-Quiz 5-5 setup), some interior nodes are labeled with prefix values and OTHERS are labeled 'none'. What does a 'none' label mean? (Figure: Fixed-stride multibit trie (stride 3) — Practice-Quiz 5-5 setup (Module 5))

options.text: The node is reserved for future RFC extensions and is never visited at runtime in any production implementation.
The node is forbidden and any packet whose trie path reaches it is dropped at the input port immediately.
The node points back to the root for retry, in case of forwarding-table corruption — multiple RFCs and BCPs prescribe this behaviour for production inter-domain routing systems.
The node has no prefix entry of its own (the database has no exact prefix terminating at this node) — though the node may still have a pointer to a subtrie below.

questions.explanation: 'none' = no prefix terminates here, but subtrie may exist. The label indicates the node has no associated next-hop entry; lookups still descend into the subtrie below if a pointer exists.

questions.prompt: Scenario in the fixed-stride trie figure: a lookup traverses to a node labeled 'none'. What value does the LOOKUP itself return? (Figure: Fixed-stride trie — lookup with no exact match at the leaf (Module 5))

options.text: The last prefix value remembered along the path before reaching the 'none' node — even though the node itself is 'none', the lookup uses the longest matched prefix it has seen so far.
'none' — if the leaf label is 'none', the lookup returns no match and the packet is dropped, reflecting how the routing decision flows through a typical commercial-grade daemon at scale.
The shortest prefix along the path is returned, mirroring the conservative LPM rule for fixed-stride tries in production environments.
Whatever prefix is stored at the root — fixed-stride lookups always return the default entry when no leaf matches exactly.

questions.explanation: Lookup remembers the longest-matched prefix along the path. As the lookup descends, every non-'none' node it passes updates the 'best match so far'; if it ends at 'none', it returns whatever was last remembered.

questions.prompt: In the fixed-stride trie figure, what does the lookup return for an address starting with bits 001? (Figure: Fixed-stride trie — looking up address 001 (Module 5))

options.text: P1 — the shortest prefix in the database is always returned when no specific subtrie matches the address.
P5 — the lookup terminates at the root with no outgoing pointer for 001 and returns P5 as the remembered match.
No match — 001 has no matching prefix in this database and the packet is dropped.
P7 — fixed-stride tries always return the most-specific prefix value in the leftmost subtrie for unknown patterns.

questions.explanation: 001 has no child of the root in this trie => return remembered match (P5). The root's pointer for bit-pattern 001 is absent, so the lookup terminates and returns whatever prefix the root itself represented (P5).

questions.prompt: In the fixed-stride trie figure, what does each trie element actually store? (Figure: Fixed-stride trie — element contents (Module 5))

options.text: Only a prefix value — pointers between trie levels are computed implicitly from the address bits.
Only a pointer — prefix values are stored centrally in a separate table outside the trie itself.
A pointer (to the next subtrie, if any) and a prefix value (if the node corresponds to a stored prefix).
A pointer and a TTL counter — the prefix value lives elsewhere in the router.

questions.explanation: Each trie element = pointer + prefix value. A node carries both a pointer to the next-stride subtrie (if any) and the prefix value associated with the path leading to it (if a prefix terminates there).

questions.prompt: How does a VARIABLE-stride multibit trie differ from a fixed-stride one, and what does it gain? (Figure: Variable-stride trie — different strides per subtrie (Module 5))

options.text: Variable-stride tries are used only for IPv6; IPv4 uses fixed-stride exclusively — this is the canonical convention documented in the standard reference for production routers.
Variable-stride tries always use exactly twice the memory of fixed-stride tries; the trade-off is faster lookup.
Variable-stride tries forbid prefix expansion, so they are always smaller than fixed-stride tries.
Each node can pick its stride independently — the optimum stride per subtrie is chosen via dynamic programming, reducing wasted entries.

questions.explanation: Variable stride: per-node stride picked via DP. Each subtrie's stride is chosen independently to minimize memory; sparse subtries get small strides, dense ones get larger strides — better than one global stride.

questions.prompt: In the variable-stride trie figure, the rightmost node uses 3 bits while the leftmost uses 2. Why the asymmetry? (Figure: Variable-stride trie — example sizing (Module 5))

options.text: The rightmost subtrie contains a prefix P7 that requires 3 bits to disambiguate, while the leftmost subtrie's longest prefix P3 fits in 2 bits.
Hardware constraints force odd-indexed nodes to use stride 3 across every variable-stride router.
Variable-stride strictly alternates 2-bit and 3-bit nodes, regardless of the underlying prefix database stored at the router.
The router's clock rate doubles on the right side of the trie, requiring fewer bits per step, as is widely deployed across modern Tier-1 networks for predictable inter-domain behaviour.

questions.explanation: Different strides because different subtries need different prefix lengths. The rightmost subtrie holds prefixes that require 3 bits to disambiguate (P7); the leftmost only needs 2 bits (P3).

questions.prompt: In a variable-stride multibit trie, the choice of stride at each node is fixed at compile time via dynamic programming to minimize total memory.
questions.explanation: Strides chosen at compile-time via DP over the prefix database. Dynamic programming computes the optimal stride choice per node before deployment; lookup itself is then deterministic for each subtrie.

questions.prompt: In the figure, why do modern routers need PACKET CLASSIFICATION on top of longest-prefix match? (Figure: Why packet classification — beyond longest-prefix match (Module 6))

options.text: Operators apply rules using multiple header fields at once (src IP, dst IP, port, protocol) for firewall, QoS and accounting decisions; LPM by itself can only match on one field.
Longest-prefix match has been deprecated by the IETF and is no longer in production deployments.

Modern hardware cannot perform longest-prefix match at line rate any more — this is the canonical convention documented in the standard reference for production routers., as is widely deployed across modern Tier-1 networks for predictable inter-domain behaviour.
Packet classification replaces routing entirely; once classification is enabled, the FIB is unused.

questions.explanation: Classification matches MULTI-field rules (firewall/QoS/accounting); LPM matches one. Operators need decisions on (src IP, dst IP, port, protocol) tuples — denying a service, queueing premium customer traffic, billing — which a single-field LPM lookup can't express.

questions.prompt: Packet classification typically matches against multiple header fields simultaneously, while plain forwarding usually matches the destination prefix only.
questions.explanation: Classification = multi-field; forwarding LPM = dst-prefix only. The two lookups live side by side: forwarding picks the output port, classification applies filtering/QoS/policy rules on additional header fields.

questions.prompt: A naïve linear scan of every classifier rule against every packet works correctly but does not scale. What is the FUNDAMENTAL bottleneck of the naïve approach?

options.text: Linear scans require atomic locks on every rule, which serializes all classification across the router.
With R rules, the worst-case lookup cost is O(R) per packet — at high R and line rate, this exhausts the per-packet time budget at every router.
Linear scans cannot be parallelized across multiple input ports, only across multiple cores.
Linear scans do not support IPv6 because of address length — operators rely on this property when designing their routing policies for global reachability.

questions.explanation: Naive scan = O(R) per packet => blows time budget. At line rate with thousands of rules, scanning every rule per packet exhausts the few-nanosecond per-packet budget — fast classification needs better data structures.

questions.prompt: In the set-pruning-trie figure, what is the data-structure idea behind 'set pruning'? (Figure: Set-pruning trie — rules replicated across multiple source tries (Module 6))

options.text: Set pruning uses a hash table per prefix length to skip subtries that have no rules across every router participating in the same routing domain at the same moment.
Set pruning removes rules that overlap with broader prefixes, reducing the total rule count exposed to the router.

Set pruning builds a destination trie and attaches a source trie at each node holding every rule whose destination prefix matches there — each rule is replicated into every destination-subtrie it could match, so once the destination lookup ends, that node's source trie already lists every applicable rule.
Set pruning re-orders rules by priority so the highest-priority rule is checked first.

questions.explanation: Set-pruning = destination trie + per-node source tries; each rule is replicated into every destination-subtrie its destination prefix could match. So when the destination lookup terminates at a node, the source trie attached there already lists every candidate rule — no further searching, at the cost of duplicating rules across many source tries.

questions.prompt: What is the main DOWNSIDE of set-pruning tries, as illustrated in the figure? (Figure: Set-pruning — memory cost vs lookup speed (Module 6))

options.text: Set-pruning tries lose the ability to perform longest-prefix match correctly.
Slow lookup compared to a linear scan, because trie traversal adds overhead.
Set-pruning tries cannot support overlapping prefixes at all, because the protocol enforces strict isolation between routing tables in the global system.
Memory blow-up — replicating rules into every relevant subtrie can multiply memory consumption far beyond the unique rule count.

questions.explanation: Memory blow-up: replicated rules multiply storage. Each rule may be stored in many subtries; for dense rule sets the memory cost can be orders of magnitude greater than the unique rule count.

questions.prompt: BACKTRACKING reduces memory compared to set-pruning. What does backtracking trade away to achieve this memory saving?

options.text: Lookup speed — instead of finding all matches at the leaf, backtracking walks back up the trie at lookup time to find missed matches.
Correctness — backtracking may miss some matching rules in pathological inputs at line rate.
IPv4 support — backtracking only works for IPv6 address layouts, which the IETF documents as the standard behaviour across all compliant implementations today.
Multi-field matching — backtracking restricts classification to a single field at most.

questions.explanation: Backtracking trades lookup speed for memory. Instead of replicating rules at compile time, lookup walks back up the trie to find missed matches — saves memory but adds variable lookup latency per packet.

questions.prompt: In the grid-of-tries figure, what does the GRID structure model? (Figure: Grid of tries — destination + source tries linked by switch pointers (Module 6))

options.text: A grid of redundant routers connected by Ethernet links for fault tolerance at scale.
A 2-D lookup over two prefix fields (e.g., src IP prefix and dst IP prefix) — one axis per field — with precomputed pointers between tries to avoid expensive backtracking.
A purely visual aid; the actual data structure is a flat hash table internally — multiple RFCs and BCPs prescribe this behaviour for production inter-domain routing systems.
A grid of TCAMs — every cell in the grid is a single TCAM entry indexed by the lookup engine.

questions.explanation: Grid of tries = 2-D lookup with precomputed cross-trie pointers. One axis per field (e.g., src prefix x dst prefix); precomputed 'switch pointers' link tries so lookup can jump between subtries without backtracking.

questions.prompt: In the grid-of-tries figure, the 'switch pointers' connecting rows let lookup avoid which expensive operation? (Figure: Grid of tries — switch pointers across rows (Module 6))

options.text: Re-allocating memory at lookup time — switch pointers are reserved memory regions used only by hardware.
Re-running longest-prefix match on every packet — switch pointers cache the result of LPM across lookups.
Full backtracking up the source-prefix trie — the precomputed switch pointer jumps directly to the next subtrie that could still match.
Re-running OSPF after every link-state change in the AS — switch pointers maintain the IGP database too.

questions.explanation: Switch pointers avoid full backtracking. Once a search runs out of matches in the inner trie, the pointer directly points to the next outer-trie subtrie that could match — turning O(W^2) backtracking into O(W) per lookup.

questions.prompt: From the HOL-blocking figure, what is head-of-line blocking in an input-queued switch? (Figure: Head-of-line blocking pattern over packet-times — take-a-ticket (Module 6))

options.text: Packets get reordered as they cross the switching fabric during congestion, reflecting how the routing decision flows through a typical commercial-grade daemon at scale.
An output queue drops packets when its buffer overflows during a microburst.
The control plane stalls while waiting for a forwarding-table update at the input port.
A packet at the front of an input queue blocks packets behind it that could otherwise be forwarded to currently-free outputs.

questions.explanation: HOL blocking: head packet pins the queue. When the first packet in an input queue can't be served (its output is busy), packets behind it cannot advance even if their outputs are idle — the input is structurally blocked.

questions.prompt: Scenario in the take-a-ticket figure: inputs A and B both want output 1, and input C wants output 2. Output 1 grants A for this round. What happens to B's packet at the head of B's input queue this round? (Figure: Take-a-ticket scheduler with HOL contention (Module 6))

options.text: B's packet stays at the head, blocking all other packets behind it in B's queue — even those that want output 2 — until B's head finally gets output 1 in a future round.
B's packet is dropped to avoid contention, and the input port sends an ICMP source-quench to the upstream link.
B's packet is automatically moved to output 2 because output 1 is busy this round — this is the canonical convention documented in the standard reference for production routers.
B's input queue is suspended for one cell time and resumes service the next round.

questions.explanation: B's head stays; everything behind it is stuck. With a single FIFO input queue, B's packet for output 1 must wait its turn; meanwhile any later packet in B's queue (even one bound for an idle output) can't be served.

questions.prompt: Why is HOL blocking BAD for throughput, even though only one packet is 'stuck' at a time? (Figure: HOL blocking — throughput impact (Module 6))

options.text: HOL blocking only affects the OUTPUT queue, leaving input queues free to drain, as is widely deployed across modern Tier-1 networks for predictable inter-domain behaviour.
The head packet pins the entire input queue, preventing other packets in that same queue from advancing toward currently-idle outputs — utilization drops.
HOL blocking just slightly increases latency but never affects throughput in any input-queued switch.
HOL blocking is unrelated to throughput; it only causes minor reordering of packets across queues.

questions.explanation: HOL pins the WHOLE input queue => utilization drops below 100%. Even though only one packet is 'blocked,' it blocks every packet behind it that could otherwise be flowing to idle outputs — leading to the famous ~58.6% throughput limit (Karol 1987).

questions.prompt: With the take-a-ticket algorithm, HOL blocking can cascade: even though many outputs may be idle, only the inputs whose head packet maps to a free output can make progress in a round. (Figure: Take-a-ticket head-of-line cascade (Module 6))
questions.explanation: Cascading HOL => many idle outputs starve. Take-a-ticket schedules one round at a time; when many inputs' heads target busy outputs, idle outputs see no traffic even though packets exist that want them.

questions.prompt: How do VIRTUAL OUTPUT QUEUES (VOQs) eliminate HOL blocking? (Figure: Virtual output queues (VOQs) — one queue per output per input (Module 6))

options.text: Inputs share a single bus to outputs in round-robin order; HOL blocking is structurally impossible on a shared bus.
Each output port runs N times faster than each input link, so traffic never queues at the input at all.
Each input maintains a separate queue per output — a packet bound for a busy output cannot block packets in its input bound for free outputs, since they sit in different queues.
Inputs maintain a single queue but dropped packets are absorbed by an in-network proxy — operators rely on this property when designing their routing policies for global reachability.

questions.explanation: VOQ = N input queues per port, one per output. A packet for a busy output sits in its own queue; other queues at the same input (for free outputs) keep advancing — no cross-output blocking.

questions.prompt: Scenario in the PIM figure: in the GRANT phase, an output receives requests from multiple inputs. What does the output do? (Figure: Parallel iterative matching (PIM) — request, grant, accept phases (Module 6))

options.text: The output rejects all the requesting inputs to avoid contention and reverts to take-a-ticket scheduling for one round.
The output sends a grant to every requesting input; the inputs decide who actually transmits.
The output queues all requests and processes them strictly in arrival order over the next several rounds.
The output picks one of the requesting inputs at random and sends a grant only to that input.

questions.explanation: Grant: each output randomly picks ONE requester. Multiple inputs may request the same output; the output randomly selects one, breaking contention probabilistically while keeping the algorithm simple.

questions.prompt: In the ACCEPT phase, assume that input A receives grants from outputs 2 AND 3. What does A do? (Figure: PIM accept phase — input receives multiple grants (Module 6))

options.text: A picks one of the granting outputs (e.g., output 2) at random and sends to that output this round.
A sends copies of the packet to BOTH outputs 2 and 3 in parallel via the crossbar fabric.
A returns both grants to the scheduler and waits another round to retry.
A drops the packet because conflicting grants indicate a misconfiguration.

questions.explanation: Accept: input picks ONE granting output (random). When multiple outputs grant the same input, the input has to choose one this round — randomized selection avoids systematic bias and keeps the algorithm distributed.

questions.prompt: Assume that inputs A and B both request output 1. Output 1 picks B in the grant phase. What does A do next? (Figure: PIM with conflicting requests (Module 6))

options.text: A is permanently blocked from output 1 by the scheduler.
A may still be granted by a different output it also requested this round; if not, A retries output 1 in a future iteration.
A and B share output 1 at half rate via time-slicing across every router participating in the same routing domain at the same moment.
A is granted output 1 anyway, since both inputs requested it.

questions.explanation: A loses output 1 this iteration but may win another. PIM iterates: if A doesn't win in this round's matching, it can be matched to a different requested output or retry output 1 in a future iteration.

questions.prompt: With VOQs and Parallel Iterative Matching, an input port can have one packet bound for a busy output without delaying packets in the same input port bound for free outputs.
questions.explanation: VOQ+PIM => no cross-output blocking. Per-output queues at each input plus iterative request-grant-accept matching can achieve near-100% throughput on uniform traffic — no HOL bottleneck.

questions.prompt: FIFO with tail-drop is the simplest output scheduler. Why isn't it 'good enough' for modern routers?

options.text: FIFO+tail-drop is patented and cannot be deployed without licensing, because the protocol enforces strict isolation between routing tables in the global system.
FIFO+tail-drop is too slow at modern line rates; hardware cannot execute it at 100 Gbps.
A single greedy flow can fill the queue, drop other flows' packets and harm fairness; FIFO+tail-drop also makes no QoS or congestion guarantees.
FIFO+tail-drop violates the end-to-end principle and is forbidden by RFC.

questions.explanation: FIFO+tail-drop = no fairness, no QoS. A greedy/aggressive flow can fill the queue and crowd out well-behaved flows; tail drop punishes whoever arrives during overflow regardless of priority or class.

questions.prompt: A 'flow' is identified using header fields (e.g., 5-tuple) and consists of packets sharing the same forwarding path and required service.
questions.explanation: Flow = packets matching same 5-tuple (or service class). Identifying flows by (src/dst IP, src/dst port, protocol) lets the scheduler give each flow its own treatment — critical for per-flow fairness and QoS.

questions.prompt: In DRR, each flow has a QUANTUM Q (bytes added to its credit per round) and a DEFICIT counter. What problem does the deficit counter solve compared to plain round robin? (Figure: Deficit Round Robin — packet queues, deficit counter, quantum size (Module 6))

options.text: The deficit counter detects HOL blocking and signals the scheduler to skip blocked flows.
The deficit counter handles TCP retransmissions; plain round-robin cannot detect them.

The deficit counter doubles throughput by allowing two packets per round per flow, which the IETF documents as the standard behaviour across all compliant implementations today. — multiple RFCs and BCPs prescribe this behaviour for production inter-domain routing systems.
