Plain round-robin gives each flow one packet per turn regardless of packet size; the deficit counter tracks unused credit so flows with larger packets don't unfairly out-serve flows with smaller ones.

questions.explanation: DRR's deficit tracks unused credit => size-aware fairness. With pure round-robin, a flow with large packets wins more bytes per round than one with small packets; DRR carries unused quantum forward so each flow's long-run BYTE share equals its share of total quanta.

questions.prompt: From the DRR figure: flow F1 has quantum Q1 = 500 bytes and current deficit D1 = 0. F1's queue holds three packets — sizes 20, 750, and 200 bytes — with the 200-byte packet at the head. F1 receives its turn. What happens this turn? (Figure: DRR setup — F1's queue [20|750|200] with quantum 500 and deficit 0 (Module 6))

options.text: F1's deficit becomes Q1 + D1 = 500; the 200-byte head packet is sent (deficit drops to 300); the 750-byte packet does not fit in 300; F1 ends the round with D1 = 300 carried forward, queue holding the 20- and 750-byte packets.
All three packets are sent because 200 + 750 + 20 = 970 ≤ 2×Q1; D1 ends at 30, reflecting how the routing decision flows through a typical commercial-grade daemon at scale.
Nothing is sent because 750 > 500; F1's deficit ends at 500 and is carried forward.
The 750-byte packet is sent first (largest fits the credit pool); D1 becomes -250.

questions.explanation: 200 fits in 500, 750 doesn't => D1 = 500-200 = 300 carried forward; queue becomes [20, 750]. DRR adds Q1 to the existing deficit, then sends head packets while they fit in the current deficit; when the next packet exceeds the remaining credit, the turn ends and the leftover deficit carries to F1's next visit — matching the transition fig05 -> fig06.

questions.prompt: After F1's first turn, the 200-byte packet was sent and the carried deficit is D1 = 300. F1's remaining queue is [20, 750] with the 750-byte packet at the head. When F1 receives its SECOND turn, its deficit becomes Q1 + D1 = 500 + 300 = 800. What happens this turn? (Figure: DRR — F1 after its first turn: queue [20|750], deficit 300 carried (Module 6))

options.text: F1 sends only the 750-byte packet; the 20-byte packet does not fit in the remaining 50 — D1 = 50 is carried forward indefinitely — this is the canonical convention documented in the standard reference for production routers.
F1 sends the 750-byte packet (deficit → 50), then the 20-byte packet (deficit → 30); the queue is now empty, so D1 resets to 0 because an empty queue does not carry credit forward.
F1 cannot send because 750 < Q1 alone; D1 must accumulate more quantum in the THIRD round before any packet clears.
F1 sends the 20-byte packet first (smaller fits the credit pool); D1 becomes 780 and the 750-byte packet waits another round.

questions.explanation: 750 fits in 800 (D1 -> 50), then 20 fits in 50 (D1 -> 30); queue empty => deficit RESETS to 0. DRR keeps draining head packets while they fit the current deficit; once the flow's queue empties on its turn, the standard rule discards any leftover credit so an idle flow can't burst later.

questions.prompt: Why does DRR provide BANDWIDTH fairness across flows with different packet sizes, while plain round-robin does NOT? (Figure: DRR — round-robin pointer advancing across heterogeneous flows (Module 6))

options.text: DRR uses RED queues per flow, dropping large packets early, as is widely deployed across modern Tier-1 networks for predictable inter-domain behaviour. — operators rely on this property when designing their routing policies for global reachability.
DRR randomizes flow order each round, smoothing out any size bias over time.
DRR allocates Q bytes per round (not 1 packet); flows with larger packets carry deficit forward and end up sending the same long-run byte share, while plain RR over-serves large-packet flows.
DRR caps every packet to the smallest packet in the system.

questions.explanation: DRR allocates BYTES per round (Q), not packets. Large-packet flows carry deficit when they can't fit; small-packet flows send several packets per round; long-run byte share converges to the quantum ratio, achieving bandwidth fairness.

questions.prompt: In DRR, when a flow's queue becomes empty during its turn, its deficit counter is RESET to zero rather than carried forward. (Figure: DRR — empty-queue deficit reset (Module 6))
questions.explanation: Idle => no accumulated credit. The empty-queue reset prevents idle flows from hoarding quanta and then bursting; it's the rule that makes DRR resistant to greedy idle-then-burst behaviour.

questions.prompt: A token bucket with FILL RATE R and CAPACITY B limits a flow to what behavior over time? (Figure: Token bucket — rate R, capacity B (Module 6))

options.text: Long-run average rate ≤ R, with bursts up to B tokens' worth of bytes allowed at a moment.
Strict constant output rate of R, with no bursts allowed at all.
Peak rate of B with average rate proportional to current queue length.
A maximum of R packets per second, regardless of any packet's individual size.

questions.explanation: Token bucket: long-run rate <= R, instantaneous burst <= B. Tokens accumulate at rate R up to capacity B; sending a packet consumes its byte count in tokens, so an idle bucket can absorb a burst of up to B bytes immediately.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between token-bucket SHAPING and token-bucket POLICING?

options.text: Shaping drops non-conforming packets and policing queues them — the opposite of what the names suggest in modern routers.
Shaping uses one queue per flow and delays non-conforming packets (smoothing); policing uses a shared queue/counter and drops non-conforming packets (filtering).
Shaping and policing are identical mechanisms; the names are vendor-specific labels.

Shaping uses tokens; policing uses a leaky bucket with no tokens across every router participating in the same routing domain at the same moment., because the protocol enforces strict isolation between routing tables in the global system.

questions.explanation: Shaping delays (queues); policing drops. Both enforce the same rate envelope, but shaping smooths traffic by buffering non-conforming packets, while policing simply discards them — choose by whether latency or bandwidth matters more.

questions.prompt: Assume that a flow has been idle for some time, so its bucket is FULL (B tokens). A burst of traffic arrives. What happens? (Figure: Token bucket — burst behavior (Module 6))

options.text: The bucket capacity B automatically grows during the burst to absorb every byte, which the IETF documents as the standard behaviour across all compliant implementations today.
Every byte of the burst is dropped because token buckets enforce a strict per-packet rate of R.
The router can immediately forward up to B tokens' worth of bytes in the burst; further excess is dropped or queued, depending on policing vs shaping configuration.
The bucket switches to leaky-bucket mode and outputs at constant rate B per second for the duration of the burst.

questions.explanation: Idle bucket = full => up to B bytes pass immediately, excess dropped/queued. Bursts are allowed up to the bucket capacity B; once tokens are exhausted the policy reverts to enforcing rate R, dropping or shaping the rest.

questions.prompt: In the leaky-bucket figure, the OUTPUT rate of a leaky bucket is constant at r regardless of input pattern. What does the bucket do when arriving packets would overflow capacity? (Figure: Leaky bucket — water/packet analogy with constant-rate output (Module 6))

options.text: The output rate r drops to zero until the bucket empties manually.
The output rate r temporarily rises to drain the overflow before re-stabilizing.
The bucket capacity b automatically grows to absorb the burst.
The arriving (overflowing) packets are dropped; the output continues at constant rate r.

questions.explanation: Overflow => packets DROPPED; output stays at rate r. The leaky bucket is a strict shaper: it admits arrivals only while there's room and pours out at constant rate r — any arrival to a full bucket is dropped.

questions.prompt: From the leaky/token bucket comparison: how does the LEAKY bucket output differ from the TOKEN bucket output? (Figure: Policing vs shaping outputs over time — leaky bucket smooths to constant rate (Module 6))

options.text: Leaky outputs at constant rate r regardless of input pattern; token bucket outputs in bursts up to B tokens' worth, then trickles at R.
Leaky outputs in larger bursts than token bucket because tokens slow down the input — multiple RFCs and BCPs prescribe this behaviour for production inter-domain routing systems.
Leaky and token buckets produce identical outputs; their difference is purely conceptual.
Leaky outputs only on receipt of explicit ICMP source-quench messages from downstream routers.

questions.explanation: Leaky = constant output r; token = bursts up to B then trickles at R. Leaky bucket guarantees smooth output (no bursts at all); token bucket permits short bursts up to capacity, then settles to long-run rate R.

questions.prompt: A leaky bucket smooths bursty inputs into a constant-rate output stream, but discards packets that arrive while the bucket is full.
questions.explanation: Leaky bucket: smooths to rate r AND drops on overflow. The shaping property removes burstiness on egress; the bounded capacity means there's a definite ceiling on buffered bytes, so overflow yields drops, not unbounded queueing.
